pillars.name: cognitive restructuring + food noise
pillars.thesis: The voice that calls foods good or bad is a script you absorbed, not your identity, and scripts can be rewritten. Naming automatic thoughts, distortions, and the moralization of food is the entry point into every other pillar.
evidenceAnchors: beck, cognitive behavior therapy for weight loss (2007)
forbiddenRegister: ' never call any food bad, clean, dirty, or treat.
never use deficit, willpower, discipline as the lever.

pillars.name: hunger, satiety, urge surfing
pillars.thesis: Hunger is data, not a moral test, and urges crest in about 90 seconds whether you eat or not. Building interoceptive literacy and the pause skill is the behavioral substrate beneath every food decision.
evidenceAnchors: brewer et al., jama internal medicine (2017)
forbiddenRegister: never override true hunger with surfing.
never frame satiety as a number — it is a felt sense.

pillars.name: all-or-nothing + self-compassion
pillars.thesis: One off-plan bite triggers full collapse only because the rule was binary, and self-criticism predicts more eating not less. Flexibility and kindness are not soft — they are the operating conditions of every maintainer.
evidenceAnchors: polivy & herman, journal of abnormal psychology (1985)
adams & leary, journal of social and clinical psychology (2007)

pillars.name: body image + identity
pillars.thesis: The body you have right now deserves the life you want, and identity is upstream of habit. Pie-chart restructuring, mirror work, and identity-based habit framing are the load-bearing techniques here.
evidenceAnchors: fairburn, cognitive behavior therapy and eating disorders (2008)
oyserman et al., journal of personality and social psychology (2006)
hershfield et al., journal of marketing research (2011)
forbiddenRegister: never tether worth to weight or shape.

pillars.name: sleep, stress, emotional regulation
pillars.thesis: Under 6 hours of sleep dysregulates appetite at the hormone level before you wake up, and stress + appetite share a single chain you can interrupt in four places. Without these the cognitive work collapses under cortisol load.
evidenceAnchors: spiegel et al., annals of internal medicine (2004)
balban et al., cell reports medicine (2023)
forbiddenRegister: no calorie math on low-sleep days as compensation.
never frame stress eating as the enemy.

pillars.name: maintenance + values
pillars.thesis: Maintenance is the program, not what comes after it. Values, not goals, direct every day; lapses are data, not verdicts; and the maintainer's life is small daily recommitment to a clear compass.
evidenceAnchors: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)
wadden et al., look ahead trial (2014)
forbiddenRegister: never frame the end of the program as a finish line.

acts.name: deconstruct the diet brain
acts.thesis: Epistemic disruption. Before any behavior change sticks, the user has to see that the diet brain she arrived with is a learned cognitive architecture — not a personal failing, not the truth. Socratic psychoeducation, restrict-binge cycle science (Polivy & Herman), thought records, and externalization. The therapeutic move is from fusion ('I have no willpower') to defusion ('I'm having the thought that I have no willpower').
acts.expectedCognitiveShift: From 'I am broken / I have no willpower' to 'the script was installed, food noise is documented neuroscience, my hunger is data, my distortions have names.' By D21 she can spot 3+ of her own distortions on sight, understands why dieting made food louder not quieter, and holds 'hunger is information' as a working frame. No behavior change prescriptions yet.

acts.name: build the skills
acts.thesis: Translate cognitive insight into behavioral architecture. The maintainer's operating system goes in: self-monitoring (Burke 2011 meta — strongest single predictor of WL across studies), implementation intentions (Gollwitzer 1999), stimulus control, urge surfing, problem-solving, sleep + stress regulation. Every skill is a personal experiment, not a prescription. Collaborative empiricism is the register.
acts.expectedCognitiveShift: From 'I need to try harder' to 'I have 5-7 skills I have personally tested, and I know my 2-3 highest-leverage ones.' By D42 the program belongs to her — she has internalized that this is HER protocol, not a generic one. She has a working pause skill, a working if-then for her hardest hour, and at least one regulator (sleep or stress) that holds.

acts.name: rewire the identity
acts.thesis: Identity work. This is the part Noom and BetterMe systematically under-teach and where the LTV unlock lives. Behavior change without identity change relapses (NWCR data, Wing & Phelan 2005 — maintainers describe themselves differently). Move from 'I am someone who is trying to lose weight' to 'I am someone whose life includes these practices.' Core belief modification via downward arrow, values clarification (ACT), self-as-context, self-compassion training (Neff), body-image CBT (Cash), future-self continuity (Hershfield).
acts.expectedCognitiveShift: From 'I am my body / my weight / my last meal' to 'I am the noticer, my body is something I have not something I am, my values direct my actions on the hard days.' By D63 she has rewritten at least one core belief, has three values she can name in her own words without thinking, and the practices have started to set as identity not effort. This is the demo lesson set the founder shows clinicians.

acts.name: maintain for life
acts.thesis: Explicit maintenance training — the part ~95% of programs skip and the variable NWCR + Look AHEAD identify as the actual determinant of outcomes at 1 year. Chronic-condition self-management posture: relapse prevention (Marlatt — lapse vs relapse), seasonal/life-event adaptation, the every-january immunity, the written maintenance plan, the written relapse plan, the long-haul relationships with hunger, the scale, the inner critic, the body that keeps changing.
acts.expectedCognitiveShift: From 'I am finishing the program' to 'the practices live in me now — the app was a scaffold, the architecture is mine.' By D84 she has a one-page written maintenance plan, a one-page relapse plan, a daily-three floor, a high-risk situation inventory, a pre-decided 24-hour response to any future lapse, and the sentence she will tell herself in next january's diet-industry surge. Graduation to chronic-condition self-management is explicit.

canonical84.workingTitle: the diet brain is [learned], not you
canonical84.evidenceAnchor: beck, cognitive behavior therapy for weight loss (2007)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: lead with 'the voice protected you, and now we're updating it' before psychoeducation.

pages.headline: the voice in your head was [taught].
pages.body: you're seven, maybe nine. someone at the table says she's being good today. someone else laughs about being bad later. a magazine has a number on it and a body next to it. you didn't decide to absorb any of this.

pages.headline: we're [not] fixing you. we're rewriting a script.
pages.body: cognitive behavior therapy starts here. the thoughts in the background, good food, bad food, started over, ruined it, are learned patterns, not personality. beck called them automatic thoughts. they fire before you consent. because they fire fast, they feel like truth.
pages.citation: beck, cognitive behavior therapy for weight loss (2007)

pages.headline: [name] the voice. just once.
pages.body: next time the script fires today, at the fridge, the mirror, the lunch order, try one move. notice it's a thought, not a fact. say in your head: that's the script. you don't have to argue. you don't have to obey. just clock that it's there. one noticing is the whole rep.
pages.prompt: what did the voice say to you the last time you opened the fridge?

pages.headline: you [learned] this voice. you can learn another.
pages.body: today is small. it's also kind of [everything]. naming the script is how every other lesson lands. tomorrow we look at why restriction made the script so loud in the first place♡

pages.headline: the voice was [protecting] you.
pages.body: somewhere along the way, the voice in your head got really good at rules. don't eat past seven. don't eat that. you've earned this, you haven't earned that. it sounds cruel from the outside. it was actually trying to keep you safe in a world that kept changing the rules on you. the voice did its job. and now, gently, we're going to update it.

pages.headline: the script was a [strategy]. it's allowed to retire.
pages.body: in cbt, the rules a person builds during a hard chapter are called protective responses. they made sense then. they're effortful and exhausting now. beck's work shows these scripts are learned patterns, not personality, and learned patterns can be rewritten one notice at a time. no demolition. just an update.
pages.citation: beck, cognitive behavior therapy for weight loss (2007)

pages.headline: [thank] the voice. then keep moving.
pages.body: the next time a rule fires today, try one sentence in your head: thank you for trying to protect me. i've got it from here. it sounds soft. it's actually how the rewrite begins.
pages.prompt: name one rule the voice has tried to keep for you.

pages.headline: the voice [served] you. you get to thank it.
pages.body: today is small. it's also kind of [everything]. we're not deleting the protector. we're letting her rest. tomorrow we look at why food got so loud in the first place♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: polivy & herman, american psychologist (1985)

cohortOverrides.glp1: food noise is pharmacologically suppressed now — when you taper this returns, so build the skill while the room is quiet.

pages.headline: it's 11pm. the pantry is [calling] your name.
pages.body: you weren't hungry an hour ago. you were doing fine. you ate a sensible dinner. you said no to the office cookie. and now you're in front of the open pantry, and the cereal box has a kind of gravity to it. you didn't fail. you didn't lack discipline.

pages.headline: restriction is what makes food [louder].
pages.body: polivy and herman ran this experiment in the 80s. people on a strict diet ate more, not less, when something disrupted the rules. counter-regulatory eating. when your brain registers a food as off limits, it gets assigned more value, not less.
pages.citation: polivy & herman, american psychologist (1985)

pages.headline: [soften] one rule. just one.
pages.body: pick the loudest off-limits food in your head right now. instead of forbidding it, try this sentence: it's allowed, and i'm choosing when. you're not eating it for the sake of it. you're taking it off the pedestal so it stops running the show.

pages.headline: the food wasn't the problem. the [rule] was.
pages.body: you don't have to prove anything by eating it today. you just have to know it's allowed. that's how the volume comes down. tomorrow we name the spiral the rules create♡

pages.headline: the room is [quiet] right now. that's the gift.
pages.body: for the first time in maybe a decade, you can walk past the pantry and not hear anything. no math. no scoring. no 11pm meeting at the cereal box. the medicine is doing real work, and your body is finally allowed to rest. and somewhere in the back of your head, you know the room won't be quiet forever. this is the chapter where we build the skill, while the volume is down.

pages.headline: restriction makes food [louder]. medicine doesn't change that.
pages.body: polivy and herman showed in the 80s that strict food rules create counter-regulatory eating. the louder the rule, the louder the want. glp-1 medications dampen the volume, but they don't rewrite the underlying script. when you taper, the script is still there, waiting. the skill we're building now is what carries you through the taper. credit the medicine. build the skill anyway.
pages.citation: polivy & herman, american psychologist (1985)

pages.headline: [soften] one rule while the room is quiet.
pages.body: pick one food the old voice called off-limits. say to yourself: it's allowed, and i'm choosing when. you don't have to want it. you don't have to eat it. you're just taking it off the pedestal so it doesn't run the show when the medicine steps back.

pages.headline: the medicine is working. the [skill] is yours.
pages.body: you get to use this quiet room to learn the moves your future self will need. that's not cheating. that's the smartest thing you've ever done. tomorrow we name the spiral the rules create♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: polivy & herman, journal of abnormal psychology (1985)

cohortOverrides.prior_attempts_high: validate first — you've felt this 100 times and called it weakness, it's a documented effect with a name.

pages.headline: you had two cookies. so [obviously] you had nine.
pages.body: it was a tuesday. someone brought cookies to work. you said one. you had two. and something happened in the eight seconds after that second cookie. a switch flipped. the day was ruined, so the day might as well be loud. you finished the cookies. went out for pasta.

pages.headline: this is a [documented] effect. it has a name.
pages.body: polivy and herman called it the what-the-hell effect. the moment a rule is broken, the brain treats the day as binary. on plan, or off plan. once off, there's no incentive to slow down until midnight resets the clock. not character.
pages.citation: polivy & herman, journal of abnormal psychology (1985)

pages.headline: [interrupt] the spiral with the next bite.
pages.body: if it happens today, try this one move. the next decision is the only decision. not what you did ten minutes ago. not what tomorrow looks like. just the next bite. that's where the spiral ends. the cookies don't get to vote on dinner.

pages.headline: you didn't [ruin] anything. you ate cookies.
pages.body: the day is not pass-fail. the day is just a day. tomorrow resets, but so does the next bite. that's the whole skill♡

pages.headline: you've felt this [a hundred] times.
pages.body: the rest of the day was already over before you finished swallowing. you've been here. monday morning, the resolve. tuesday lunch, the wobble. tuesday night, the unraveling. wednesday, starting over. you've called yourself weak. you've called it self-sabotage. you've sat with it for years thinking it was a flaw in you. it isn't. it's a documented effect, and what you felt was the effect, not your character.

pages.headline: you didn't fail. the [model] did.
pages.body: polivy and herman named this in 1985 — the what-the-hell effect. the second a rule is broken, the brain reads the day as binary: on plan or off plan. it isn't unique to you, and it isn't moral. it's the predictable output of treating eating as pass-fail. the women who stop spiraling aren't more disciplined. they stopped using a model that demanded perfection in the first place.
pages.citation: polivy & herman, journal of abnormal psychology (1985)

pages.headline: [interrupt] the spiral with the next bite.
pages.body: if it happens today, try one move. the next decision is the only decision. not what you ate at noon. not what monday should look like. just the next bite. the cookies don't get to vote on dinner. the spiral ends one decision in.

pages.headline: the past attempts were [data], not verdicts.
pages.body: every spiral you survived taught you what the model couldn't hold. that's not failure. that's research. tomorrow resets, but so does the next bite♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: beck, cognitive therapy: basics and beyond (2011)

pages.headline: the thought [fired] before you opened your eyes.
pages.body: this morning, before you were fully awake, something in your head did a small calculation. what you ate last night. how the leggings might feel. whether today gets to be good or make-up. you didn't choose that thought. it ran on its own, like an app you forgot was installed.

pages.headline: the [abc] model. it's how you catch a thought.
pages.body: this morning, between the alarm and your feet on the floor, something in your head ran a calculation. what you ate last night. how the leggings might feel. whether you'd earned coffee. you didn't write that thought.
pages.citation: beck, cognitive therapy: basics and beyond (2011)

pages.headline: [catch] one thought today. write it down.
pages.body: when a feeling hits you sideways today, pause for ten seconds. ask: what just went through my head? that's the b. you don't have to fix it or argue with it. you just have to see it. the seeing is the whole skill.
pages.prompt: write down one automatic thought you caught today.

pages.headline: you have thoughts. you [aren't] them.
pages.body: today you learned to see the app running in the background. that's not nothing. that's the whole start. tomorrow we look at the shapes thoughts come in♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: burns, feeling good (1980)

pages.headline: the thoughts come in [shapes]. there are about ten.
pages.body: you've thought every one of these. i always do this. i'll never get it right. one bad meal means a bad week. she's not texting back because she's mad. if i'm not perfect, what's the point. they don't feel like patterns when you're inside them. they feel like truth.

pages.headline: burns named [ten] distortions. food brain runs on three.
pages.body: david burns wrote feeling good in 1980 and named ten distortions. most women in this work run on the same three. all-or-nothing. one bite, day ruined. labeling. not i did a lazy thing, but i'm lazy. fortune telling. this won't work, like last time.
pages.citation: burns, feeling good (1980)

pages.headline: [name] the shape when it fires.
pages.body: today, when a sticky thought lands, try labeling it by shape. that's all-or-nothing. that's fortune telling. that's a label. you don't argue. you don't fix. you just name the shape and keep moving. it's almost embarrassing how well it works.

pages.headline: you saw the [shape]. that's the whole move.
pages.body: naming the distortion takes maybe four seconds. four seconds is enough to keep the day from tipping. that's the skill. tomorrow we move from thoughts to the body♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: tribole & resch, intuitive eating (1995/2020)

cohortOverrides.glp1: signals are blunted now — practice the scale so the dial is recalibrated when you taper.
cohortOverrides.restrictive: gentle pacing, no calorie mentions in the micro-skill.

pages.headline: you don't know what [hungry] feels like anymore.
pages.body: somewhere between the smoothie cleanse and the meal-prep era, the signal got fuzzy. you eat by the clock. you eat by the rule. and when your body sends a small, specific message, i'd like food, in about an hour, you talk over it. it's been talked over for years.

pages.headline: hunger is [data]. there's a 0-10 scale.
pages.body: tribole and resch built the hunger-fullness scale. zero is empty and shaky. ten is uncomfortably full. five is neutral. the work is eating mostly between a three (polite gurgle) and a six (satisfied, not heavy). the scale doesn't tell you what to eat.
pages.citation: tribole & resch, intuitive eating (1995/2020)

pages.headline: [check in] twice. before you eat. after.
pages.body: before lunch, ask: where am i on 0-10? after lunch, twenty minutes later, ask again. you're not grading. you're collecting data. one week of this and you'll start to notice the dial coming back online.

pages.headline: your body has been [talking]. we're listening now.
pages.body: the signal was always there. it just got quiet under all the rules. softening the rules turns the signal back up. tomorrow we look at why the signal got quiet in the first place♡

pages.headline: the signal is [blunted] right now. that's fine.
pages.body: you might not feel much hunger at all right now. the medicine is doing exactly what it's supposed to do. for the first time in maybe ever, the volume on the want is down. and that means the practice we do today is a little different. we're not waiting for the loud signal. we're learning to read the very quiet one, so when the dial comes back up, you already know the scale.

pages.headline: the 0-10 scale is the [dial]. practice while it's quiet.
pages.body: tribole and resch built the hunger-fullness scale: 0 is empty, 10 is uncomfortably full, the middle is where most maintainers live. on glp-1, signals are dampened. that doesn't mean the scale is useless. it means you're calibrating a quieter dial. when you taper and the signal returns, the dial will already feel familiar. you're investing in future-you, while the room is quiet.
pages.citation: tribole & resch, intuitive eating (1995/2020)

pages.headline: [check in] twice. even when it's faint.
pages.body: before lunch, ask: where am i on 0-10? after lunch, twenty minutes later, ask again. it might be a 3 to a 6, or a 4 to a 5. the number isn't the point. the noticing is. you're keeping the muscle warm.

pages.headline: you're [calibrating] now for later.
pages.body: the medicine is doing its job. you're doing yours. these are not the same job, and they go beautifully together. tomorrow we look at why the dial got quiet in the first place♡

pages.headline: the signal got [quiet] for a reason.
pages.body: for a long time, hunger was something you negotiated with. you learned to talk over it, to push it past lunch, to call it discomfort and move on. that wasn't a flaw in you. it was a skill your body built to get through a chapter. today we're not asking you to feel everything at once. we're just opening the door a crack and seeing what's there.

pages.headline: hunger is [data], not a test.
pages.body: tribole and resch made the hunger-fullness scale to give the signal a vocabulary again. zero is empty. ten is uncomfortable. the middle is the goal. the scale isn't a rule to enforce. it's a way to listen. for people whose signal got quiet under restriction, the work is slow, and gentle, and assisted by structure — meals at regular times, even when nothing speaks up yet.
pages.citation: tribole & resch, intuitive eating (1995/2020)

pages.headline: [notice] one moment. that's enough.
pages.body: pick one meal today. before you eat, just ask: is there anything here at all? a flicker, a 2, a 1. no judgment, no scoring. you're letting your body know the door is open. that's a big day, even if the room is still quiet.

pages.headline: your body is allowed to [tell] you again.
pages.body: this part is slow on purpose. nothing has to come back online today. you just opened the door. that's the whole move♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: sumithran et al., new england journal of medicine (2011)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: skip the self-blame frame entirely — lead with your body did the right thing.

pages.headline: you didn't [fail] them. they failed you.
pages.body: count them. the one in college. after the breakup. the one with the points. the one with the app. each felt like a personal verdict. it wasn't. your body did what bodies do under restriction. it wasn't weakness. it was biology. you didn't [fail] them. they failed you.

pages.headline: restriction changes the [hormones]. for months. on purpose.
pages.body: sumithran's team measured hunger and fullness hormones in people who lost weight on a restrictive diet. a year later, ghrelin was still high and leptin was still low. the body was defending the lost weight by making food noise louder. not a failure of character.
pages.citation: sumithran et al., new england journal of medicine (2011)

pages.headline: [reframe] one diet you blamed yourself for.
pages.body: pick one diet you ended in shame. now tell it back to yourself with the biology in the room. you didn't fail it. it asked your body to do something it was designed to resist. that's a different sentence than the one you've been carrying.
pages.prompt: write the new sentence down. keep it.

pages.headline: you carried the [blame]. you don't anymore.
pages.body: this is the part of the work that should have come first, years ago. it didn't. so it comes today. set the blame down. it was never yours♡

pages.headline: your body did the [right] thing.
pages.body: i want to start somewhere different today. forget every story you've been told about what was wrong with you when the rules got tight. your body kept you safe. when food got scarce, it slowed everything down. when food felt unsafe, it held on harder. it was doing its job. and the fact that you're reading this means it worked. nothing about that was a flaw.

pages.headline: the body [defends] energy. that's not a glitch.
pages.body: sumithran's team showed that after restriction, hunger and fullness hormones stayed shifted for at least a year. the body raised ghrelin and lowered leptin to make food more salient. this is a protective response, not a malfunction. for people who lived through restriction, this knowledge can sit gently. your body wasn't betraying you. it was guarding you.
pages.citation: sumithran et al., new england journal of medicine (2011)

pages.headline: [thank] your body. one specific way.
pages.body: today, find one specific thing your body has done lately. carried you to a friend. healed a scrape. let you sleep through a hard night. thank it for that thing, not for how it looks. that's the whole exercise.
pages.prompt: write one thank-you to your body today.

pages.headline: your body has been [on your team].
pages.body: we're going slowly here on purpose. nothing has to change today. just let your body have the credit it earned♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: marlatt & gordon, relapse prevention (1985)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: do not use to override true hunger — between-meal urges only with no physical hunger.

pages.headline: your hand is already on the [drawer].
pages.body: it's 2:47. you're not hungry. a spreadsheet is open behind you, and it's deeply boring. the office snack drawer is reliable. it asks nothing of you. somewhere between the thought and the open-the-drawer there's a window. maybe five seconds. you've been speedrunning past it your whole life. today we're going to live inside it for a minute.

pages.headline: the urge is a [wave], not a prophecy.
pages.body: marlatt called this urge surfing. an urge rises, peaks, and falls, often within about 90 seconds, whether you act on it or not. when you ride the wave instead of obeying it, the brain learns the urge isn't a command. it's a sensation. obey, the wave gets taller.
pages.citation: Update citation to 'bowen & marlatt, psychology of addictive behaviors (2009)' — the actual urge-surfing RCT.

pages.headline: [breathe] through one wave. one time.
pages.body: next non-hunger urge that hits, try one cyclic sigh. big inhale through the nose, a second small inhale on top, long exhale through the mouth. three rounds. about 90 seconds. then check if the urge is still where you left it. surprisingly often, it isn't. urges rise, peak, fall in minutes.
pages.breath_line: double-inhale through the nose. long exhale through the mouth. three rounds.

pages.headline: you don't have to [obey] every wave.
pages.body: you can surf one. you can eat the next one. the point isn't perfection. the point is knowing the window is there. tomorrow we step back from the thought itself♡

pages.headline: this skill is for [between] meals. not instead of them.
pages.body: before we talk about urge surfing, the first move is to say what it isn't. it isn't a tool to override real hunger. if your body is at a 3 on the scale, asking for food, that's not an urge to ride. that's a meal to eat. today's skill is for the in-between moments — the boredom drawer, the 11pm spiral when dinner was a full meal. that's where the window lives.

pages.headline: the urge is a [wave], not a verdict.
pages.body: marlatt called this urge surfing. an urge rises and falls in about 90 seconds, whether you act on it or not. when used for non-hunger moments, riding the wave teaches your brain that the sensation isn't a command. used in place of actual hunger, the same tool tips into restriction. so we keep the distinction crisp: meals are not urges. urges are for in-between.
pages.citation: marlatt & gordon, relapse prevention (1985)

pages.headline: [breathe] through one in-between wave.
pages.body: after a full meal today, if a non-hunger urge shows up, try one cyclic sigh: a big inhale through the nose, a second small inhale on top, a long exhale through the mouth. three rounds. about 90 seconds. that's it.
pages.breath_line: double-inhale through the nose. long exhale through the mouth. three rounds.

pages.headline: real hunger always gets [answered].
pages.body: this skill is small and specific. between-meal waves only. your meals stay sacred. tomorrow we step back from the thought itself♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes et al., acceptance and commitment therapy (1999)

pages.headline: the thought said you've [ruined] it. you believed it.
pages.body: saturday afternoon. you ate something you weren't planning to eat. and a sentence ran through your head. i've ruined it. you didn't say it out loud. you took it at face value, the way you'd take a fact off a label. then the afternoon got loud.

pages.headline: the thought is [in] you. it isn't you.
pages.body: hayes built a tool inside ACT for this. it's called defusion. instead of arguing with the thought, you step back from it. you don't say no, i haven't ruined it. you say. i'm having the thought that i've ruined it. a small grammar change.
pages.citation: hayes et al., acceptance and commitment therapy (1999)

pages.headline: [reword] one sticky thought today.
pages.body: next sticky thought that hits, try it. i'm having the thought that i'll never get it together. i'm noticing the thought that today is ruined. you don't have to disagree. you just have to put the phrase in front of it. the space is the whole point.

pages.headline: the thought [passed through]. you stayed.
pages.body: Add pull-quote: 'thoughts are weather. you are climate.' Set as italic-Fraunces 22pt with hairline cocoa rules at 40pt width above and below.

canonical84.workingTitle: good food bad food is a [category] error
canonical84.evidenceAnchor: satter, secrets of feeding a healthy family (2008)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: habituation work — clinician supervision recommended for the 'eat the bad food' micro-skill.

pages.headline: the bread isn't [good]. the bread isn't bad.
pages.body: you're at a restaurant with friends. the bread basket lands. and your brain immediately puts it in a column. bad, off limits, off plan. someone else at the table eats a piece without a single sentence happening in their head. they're not better than you.

pages.headline: food is [food]. morality doesn't apply.
pages.body: ellyn satter spent decades on this with families. when a food is forbidden, it gets assigned moral weight, which makes it more rewarding, not less. eating it becomes a transgression, which triggers shame, which fuels more eating.
pages.citation: satter, secrets of feeding a healthy family (2008)

pages.headline: [rename] one food today.
pages.body: pick one food you call bad. drop the label. don't replace it with good. just call it what it is — bread, pasta, ice cream, cheese. it sounds tiny. it does something. the label is what gives the food the voltage.

pages.headline: you eat food. you [aren't] food.
pages.body: nothing you ate today made you good or bad. nothing you eat tomorrow will. that's the deal. tomorrow we look at the number you weigh yourself by♡

pages.headline: this one we do [slowly]. with support.
pages.body: for some of us, the list of forbidden foods isn't a habit. it's a fence that's been holding the harder feelings out. today's lesson is about taking the morality off the food. and i want to say clearly: if you've been working through a restrictive history, this is the kind of work that goes better with a clinician at your side. you can read this. you don't have to do it alone, and you don't have to do it today.

pages.headline: the label gives the food its [voltage].
pages.body: ellyn satter's work showed that forbidden foods get assigned more reward, not less. for people without restriction history, the antidote is gentle exposure — calling the food what it is. for people with restriction history, exposure is real clinical work and it's usually done in steps, with support. either way, the principle is the same: morality isn't a useful category for flour. but the pacing is different, and yours is allowed to be slow.
pages.citation: satter, secrets of feeding a healthy family (2008)

pages.headline: [notice] one moral label. don't act on it yet.
pages.body: today's move is small. just notice one moment your brain assigns good or bad to a food. you don't have to eat anything new. you don't have to challenge the label. just see it. if and when you want to take the next step, do it with someone who knows your history.

pages.headline: the [pace] is yours. always.
pages.body: this work belongs to you, and it belongs to your team. nothing has to be brave today. tomorrow we look at the number you weigh yourself by♡

canonical84.workingTitle: the scale is [one] number among many
canonical84.evidenceAnchor: helander et al., journal of medical internet research (2014)

cohortOverrides.perimenopause: estrogen tide shifts the weekly read by 1-2 lbs — read 28-day trend, mark your lowest cycle week as baseline.

pages.headline: sunday morning. four seconds. one [number].
pages.body: before coffee, before anyone, before you've decided what kind of person you are today. you step on the scale. the number comes up. and in about four seconds, you've decided what the whole week gets to feel like. one number. seven days. that's a trade your brain made without asking you, and today we interrupt it.

pages.headline: the scale is [weather]. the trend is climate.
pages.body: your weight can swing up to a kilo across a day. water, sodium, your cycle, when you last ate, how you slept. helander's team studied daily weighing and found the people who handled it best treated single readings as noise and watched the 28-day line.
pages.citation: helander et al., journal of medical internet research (2014)
pages.data_tie: your 28-day weight trend (becoming tab) is the line we read together.

pages.headline: [feed] the line. don't read the day.
pages.body: next weigh-in, try one move. log it and look away. don't let the number narrate your morning. you weighed in to feed the line, not to get graded by it. the line is the only reader that has any signal.

pages.headline: the scale doesn't get to [vote] on your week.
pages.body: the trend gets a voice. you get the deciding one. that's the whole hierarchy. tomorrow we look at where all-or-nothing hides♡

pages.headline: the [tide] moves the number. it isn't your week.
pages.body: you step on the scale. it's up 1.5 pounds. nothing about your week explains it. but estrogen has been shifting in ways nobody warned you about, and your body holds water differently across the cycle now. the number isn't lying. it just isn't measuring what you think it's measuring. today we read the tide instead of the wave.

pages.headline: the cycle moves it [1-2 lbs]. read the 28-day line.
pages.body: helander's team studied daily weighing and found the people who handled it best read the line, not the day. in perimenopause and across the menstrual cycle, hormonal shifts can move weight by 1 to 2 pounds in either direction without any change in body composition. the workaround is structural: take your weekly read at the same point in your cycle when possible, and compare 28-day trend to 28-day trend, not week to week.
pages.citation: helander et al., journal of medical internet research (2014)
pages.data_tie: your 28-day weight trend (becoming tab) holds the climate. the day is just the weather.

pages.headline: [mark] your lowest week. that's your baseline.
pages.body: this week, notice the days you usually weigh in lowest in your cycle. that's your honest baseline. read the trend against trend, not point against point. when the tide rises, you don't have to take it personally.

pages.headline: your body is [keeping time]. let it.
pages.body: the number is noisier now. the line is honest. you get to read the line. tomorrow we look at where all-or-nothing hides♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: fairburn, cognitive behavior therapy and eating disorders (2008)

pages.headline: all-or-nothing isn't [one] habit. it's three.
pages.body: it shows up at the breakfast table when you call the day ruined. it shows up at the gym when you skip because you only have twenty minutes. it shows up sunday night when you say it'll all start tomorrow. same pattern, three different costumes. once you see all three, you can interrupt at the costume change, not just the meltdown.

pages.headline: binary thinking lives in [food], movement, and time.
pages.body: fairburn mapped this in cbt for eating disorders. dichotomous thinking shows up across three domains. food (on plan or off). movement (a real workout or it doesn't count). time (started, or starting monday). same brain pattern, three life areas.
pages.citation: fairburn, cognitive behavior therapy and eating disorders (2008)

pages.headline: [map] your three. write them down.
pages.body: in food, where is your line between on-plan and off? in movement, what counts as a real workout and what doesn't? in time, when does this week stop counting and next week start? write one sentence for each. you're not fixing them today. you're seeing them.
pages.prompt: name your three lines: food, movement, time.

pages.headline: you saw the [pattern]. it can't run unseen now.
pages.body: twelve days in. you can name automatic thoughts, distortions, the wave, the scale, the lines. that's not small. tomorrow we keep building♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: young et al., schema therapy (2003)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: gentle pacing, optional skip with reframe.

pages.headline: your inner critic has a [script]
pages.body: you ate the thing. or you didn't. either way she shows up. the voice that says you're disgusting, that everyone can tell, that you'll never get it together. notice this. she uses the same five sentences. she has since you were twelve.

pages.headline: old wiring, repeated until it [sounded] like you
pages.body: in schema therapy we call these early scripts schemas. the punitive parent. the defectiveness story. the unrelenting standards. they're not personality. they're wiring laid down before you had words for it, then reinforced every time the world rhymed with them.
pages.citation: Update citation to 'gilbert, compassion focused therapy (2009)' — compassionate-other externalization is the correct tradition for a single-lesson critic exercise, not Young schema therapy which is a 1-2 year modality.

pages.headline: write her [five] favorite sentences
pages.body: open notes. write the five things the critic says most often. exact words. don't soften. don't defend. just transcribe her, like you're catching a voicemail. seeing them on a screen, side by side, does something arguing never could. they start to look like what they are. a script.
pages.prompt: what are her five favorite sentences? write them as quotes.

pages.headline: she's a [roommate]. not the landlord.
pages.body: i used to think her voice was the adult in the room. she isn't. she's a roommate i didn't pick, running an old script. tomorrow we learn how to talk back to her with the only thing she's never heard. kindness.

pages.headline: your inner critic has a [script]
pages.body: before we name her, we name what's true. your body grew a person. it fed a person. it is doing the work of recovering from something enormous. the critic showing up right now isn't a sign you're broken. it's a sign an old script is trying to make sense of a body that has changed. okay so here's the thing. she uses the same five sentences. she did before the baby. she will after. that's not your voice. that's wiring.

pages.headline: old wiring, louder right [now]
pages.body: schema therapy calls these scripts early maladaptive schemas. postpartum, sleep-deprived, hormonally shifting, they get loud. that's not weakness. that's the script meeting a body it doesn't recognize and panicking. the work isn't to silence her. it's to notice she's running the same five lines she always has, and that the body she's narrating is, right now, doing one of the hardest things a body does.
pages.citation: young et al., schema therapy (2003)

pages.headline: write her [five] favorite sentences
pages.body: open notes. write the five things she says most often about your postpartum body. exact words. don't argue. just transcribe. when you see them written down, you'll notice she's recycling. that's the whole point. she's old code meeting a brand new situation.
pages.prompt: what are her five favorite sentences? write them as quotes.

pages.headline: she's a [roommate]. you're the body that built a person.
pages.body: she's loud right now. she's also wrong. tomorrow we learn how to talk back with the one thing she's never heard. a body that did this much deserves it.

pages.headline: your inner critic has a [script]
pages.body: before we go any further, a soft check. if the critic's voice runs through food and bodies with a force that hurts to be inside, today is one to do with a real person. a therapist, a dietitian, the neda helpline at 1-800-931-2237. you can skip this one and come back. okay so here's the small piece that's safe to know. the voice uses the same five sentences. it's a script. it isn't you. that part is true whether we work it today or in a room with someone who can hold it with you.

pages.headline: scripts get loudest where they were [built]
pages.body: schema therapy calls these scripts early maladaptive schemas. for some of us the food and body schema was built early and reinforced hard. so the voice around food is the loudest one in the house. that doesn't mean you're broken. it means the wiring is sensitive there, and sensitive wiring deserves trained hands. the part that's yours to know: the script is not the same as the truth.
pages.citation: young et al., schema therapy (2003)

pages.headline: this one is [optional]
pages.body: you can write the five sentences if it feels okay. you can also close the app and text someone safe. you can also skip and come back. all three are the lesson. choosing what your nervous system can carry today is the skill.
pages.prompt: if it feels safe: what are her five favorite sentences? if not: who is one safe person you can text right now?

pages.headline: soft pacing is the [skill]
pages.body: you didn't owe yourself a hard one today. tomorrow is about self-compassion, and you're already practicing it by noticing what was too much. that's not avoidance. that's wisdom.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: adams & leary, journal of social and clinical psychology (2007)

pages.headline: self-compassion is not [self-indulgence]
pages.body: here's the part i had to learn the hard way. for years i thought if i went easy on myself, i'd never change. the only fuel that worked was contempt. then a therapist asked me what i'd say to my little sister if she ate the thing.

pages.headline: kindness predicts [less] eating, not more
pages.body: adams and leary ran the study everyone references. they had restrained eaters break their rule with donuts. half got a self-compassion message. the other half got nothing. then both groups were offered candy. the self-compassion group ate less. self-criticism doesn't motivate.
pages.citation: adams & leary, journal of social and clinical psychology (2007)

pages.headline: the [sister] move
pages.body: next time the critic shows up, pause for one breath and ask: what would i say to a sister i love who just did this exact thing? say that. out loud if you can. it'll feel fake the first time. it isn't. it's new wiring. neff's three pieces: this is hard, hard happens to humans, may i be kind to myself right now.

pages.headline: the kind voice [wins] the long game
pages.body: contempt is fast and loud. kindness is slow and stays. you don't have to feel it yet. you just have to say it once today, and let your nervous system notice nobody died♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: clinician-supported only — surface NEDA helpline link.

pages.headline: the diet cycle on [paper]
pages.body: every diet you've done has had the same four corners. you just couldn't see them because you were inside them. rule. break. shame. restart. monday rolls in. the rule gets tighter to make up for the break. the rule gets broken. shame floods. monday rolls in. okay so today we draw it. once it's on paper it stops being your personality.

pages.headline: the loop is [drawable]. that's the whole trick.
pages.body: cooper, fairburn and hawker built the cbt-obesity model around this. you and the clinician collaboratively draw the cycle that keeps you stuck. not to blame you. to see it. in your head it looks like character. on paper it looks like a system, and systems can be edited.
pages.citation: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: draw your [four] corners
pages.body: open notes or paper. write four words at four corners: rule, break, shame, restart. under each, write the exact version yours takes. what's the rule. what counts as breaking. what shame sentence shows up. what monday looks like. then circle the corner that feels softest to interrupt first.
pages.prompt: which corner did you circle, and why that one?

pages.headline: seeing the loop is half the [exit]
pages.body: you've been running this for years without a map. now there's a map. tomorrow we talk about why willpower never worked, and what does.

pages.headline: this one is best done [together]
pages.body: today's lesson asks you to map the rule-break-shame-restart loop on paper. for some of us that map is too close to disordered patterns to draw alone. so here's my ask. if food rules have ever felt like more than a phase, save this lesson for a session with a therapist or dietitian, or call neda at 1-800-931-2237 to get matched. the work is still yours. you just deserve hands on it with you.

pages.headline: collaborative is the [point]
pages.body: cooper, fairburn and hawker built this technique to be done collaboratively with a clinician, not solo. that isn't a footnote. that's the point. mapping the loop alone can pull the patterns closer instead of giving distance from them. doing it with a trained person is what makes the loop loosen instead of tighten.
pages.citation: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: the [skill] today is the pause
pages.body: not doing the worksheet alone is the skill. notice if any part of you wanted to push through anyway. that part is the same part the rest of the program is gently retraining. you can come back to this lesson with someone. that's not a delay. that's the right pacing.
pages.prompt: who could you bring this lesson to? write one name.

pages.headline: knowing what's [yours] alone is the work
pages.body: you don't have to do every lesson the same way. choosing what your nervous system can hold without a hand in it is its own intelligence. tomorrow's lesson is yours to take.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hofmann et al., journal of personality (2012)

pages.headline: willpower is a [myth], structure isn't
pages.body: you tell yourself this monday is different because you'll be more disciplined. then thursday at 4pm a co-worker brings in the box, the box opens, your hand opens, and you decide you have no character. you didn't lack willpower. you lacked structure.

pages.headline: the people with the [least] desire resisted most
pages.body: hofmann's team paged 205 adults seven times a day for a week to ask what they were resisting and how they did. people we call self-controlled didn't fight more urges. they had fewer urges to fight. their lives were arranged so temptation never showed up at full volume.
pages.citation: hofmann et al., journal of personality (2012)

pages.headline: redesign [one] room
pages.body: pick one. the snack drawer at work. the kitchen counter. the nightstand. ask: what's currently within arm's reach when i'm tired? move two things. add one thing that fits what you actually want more of. tiny. boring. that's structure. structure is what willpower looks like from the inside.

pages.headline: you weren't [weak]. you were unfurnished.
pages.body: every monday you started with discipline. tomorrow you start with a redesigned counter. one is a feeling. the other is a fact. facts hold.

pages.headline: structure, not [rules]
pages.body: today's lesson is about environment design, and i want to be careful with it. for some of us, redesigning the kitchen has been code for restriction. that's not what we're after. we're after a room where all foods stay welcome and the ones you want more of are easier to see. okay so here's the soft version. flexibility is the structure. the goal isn't to make food harder. it's to make ease easier.

pages.headline: low willpower load, not low [food]
pages.body: hofmann's study found that the people who looked most disciplined weren't fighting more urges. they were living with fewer urges to fight. for a brain with a restrictive history, that does not mean fewer foods in the house. that means fewer punishing rules in the head. the structure we're building is a room where eating is allowed and choosing is light.
pages.citation: hofmann et al., journal of personality (2012)

pages.headline: add [one] thing, don't remove
pages.body: the rule for you today is additive. add one food you want more of to a place you'll see it. a bowl of fruit on the counter, a snack you actually enjoy at eye level. notice if a part of you wanted the lesson to be about removing things. that part is the script we're retiring.

pages.headline: flexibility is the [structure]
pages.body: you weren't undisciplined. you were over-ruled. one less rule, one more permission. that's the redesign.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: macht, appetite (2008)

pages.headline: emotional eating is [information]
pages.body: it's tuesday, 9pm, the kitchen light is the only one on, and you're eating crackers you don't even like. you tell yourself you're disgusting. that voice is loud. but underneath it, if you got quiet for a second, there's a different sentence.

pages.headline: every eating episode has a [function]
pages.body: macht's review of the appetite literature draws the line. emotional eating isn't a moral failing. it's a behavior with a function. comfort, distraction, sedation, regulation of an internal state your nervous system couldn't process any other way.
pages.citation: macht, appetite (2008)

pages.headline: ask the [three]-word question
pages.body: next time you catch yourself eating without hunger, pause for one breath and ask. what am i feeling. one word answers are fine. tired. lonely. wired. annoyed. you don't have to do anything with the answer today. naming the feeling is the lesson.

pages.headline: the food was carrying a [message]
pages.body: you weren't broken at the cracker drawer. you were communicating with yourself the only way you knew. tomorrow we talk about the body that was holding it all.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cash, the body image workbook (2008)

cohortOverrides.postpartum: lead with body-changed validation — postpartum bodies are baseline, not deviation.
cohortOverrides.perimenopause: validate the body that is, not the body that was.

pages.headline: the body you're in [right now]
pages.body: you got dressed this morning and the mirror caught you. for a second, before the script, there was just a body. a chest that holds your heart. legs that got you to the kitchen. hands. then the script arrived and started narrating what was wrong.

pages.headline: neutrality before [positivity]
pages.body: cash's body-image work doesn't ask you to love your body. it asks you to stop fighting it. notice the appearance thought, name it as a thought not a fact, then return attention to what the body is doing rather than how it looks. function before form.
pages.citation: cash, the body image workbook (2008)

pages.headline: one [function] sentence in the mirror
pages.body: next time you pass a mirror, instead of cataloguing, say one sentence about what this body is doing. she got me here. she's breathing. she carried groceries up three flights yesterday. it'll feel performative. that's because the script in your head is a professional. the new line is an amateur. let her practice.

pages.headline: this body is the [one] doing the work
pages.body: you don't have to love her yet. you just have to stop briefing the critic on her every time you walk past glass. tomorrow we talk about why diets broke this relationship in the first place.

pages.headline: the postpartum body is the [baseline]
pages.body: you got dressed this morning and the mirror caught you. and the first thought wasn't a thought. it was a comparison. to a body that doesn't live here anymore. okay so before we go further, i want to say the part most apps won't. your postpartum body isn't a deviation from the truer version of you. it is the version of you that grew a person. that's the baseline now. we are not snapping anything back.

pages.headline: grief is allowed. so is [neutrality].
pages.body: cash's body-image work makes room for both. you can grieve that the body changed and refuse the script that says the change is a problem to solve. notice the appearance thought, name it as a thought not a fact, return attention to what this body is doing today. it's doing a lot. function before form. forced positivity isn't the goal. softness is.
pages.citation: cash, the body image workbook (2008)

pages.headline: one [function] sentence in the mirror
pages.body: next time you pass a mirror, say one sentence about what this body is doing right now. she fed the baby. she held him through the 3am. she's still here. it'll feel small. it isn't. small is the entire instrument.

pages.headline: this body is the [baseline]. not a draft.
pages.body: she doesn't owe anyone a before-photo. tomorrow we talk about why the math diet culture sold you was always wrong.

pages.headline: the body that [is], not the body that was
pages.body: you got dressed this morning and a body you weren't expecting was in the mirror. weight settled differently. sleep is doing what it's doing. and a voice that's been with you since your twenties is treating this like a failure of effort. okay so here's the part nobody tells you. perimenopause changes the body for reasons that have nothing to do with what you ate yesterday. and the body in front of you is the one we're building with.

pages.headline: neutrality before [positivity]
pages.body: cash's body-image work doesn't ask you to love this body. it asks you to stop fighting the body that exists today as if it were the body of ten years ago. notice the appearance thought, name it as a thought not a fact, return to function. the body that is, not the body that was. comparison to past you is the most expensive habit on the menu, and we're putting it down.
pages.citation: cash, the body image workbook (2008)

pages.headline: one [function] sentence in the mirror
pages.body: next time you pass glass, say one sentence about what this body is doing now. she walked. she's working. she carried me through a hot flash at lunch. function-language is what re-roots the relationship. the past-tense version of you doesn't live here. she doesn't get the vote.

pages.headline: this body is the [one] we work with
pages.body: she isn't a draft of someone else. tomorrow we look at why the diets you tried before kept missing this exact moment.

pages.headline: the body you're in [right now]
pages.body: before we go further, a soft check. mirror work and body-image lessons can pull old wiring close for some of us. if the relationship with the body in the glass has ever felt like a war you couldn't put down, today is a lesson to do with a real person. a therapist, a dietitian, or the neda helpline at 1-800-931-2237. you can skip and come back. choosing not to white-knuckle this is the skill.

pages.headline: neutrality is the [floor]
pages.body: cash's body-image work asks for neutrality first, not positivity. for a brain with a restrictive history, that floor is the right ambition. notice the appearance thought, name it as a thought not a fact, return attention to function. doing this with trained hands on it is what makes the floor hold.
pages.citation: cash, the body image workbook (2008)

pages.headline: the [skill] today is the choice
pages.body: you can try the mirror sentence if it feels okay. you can also close the app. you can also text someone safe. the choice itself is the practice. you are allowed to take care of yourself in a way that doesn't perform progress.

pages.headline: soft pacing keeps you in the [room]
pages.body: tomorrow's lesson is yours to take whenever it fits. the program will be here. so will you.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: mann et al., american psychologist (2007)

cohortOverrides.glp1: pharmacology shifts the math but does not eliminate it — the skills are the bridge.

pages.headline: the [rebound] math
pages.body: you've done this before. you lost it. you couldn't keep it off. and the script says it's because you stopped caring. here's the part nobody told you. the regain wasn't a verdict on your character. it was the predictable second half of a math problem you were never shown.

pages.headline: the body [defends] a lower weight
pages.body: mann's team reviewed 31 long-term diet studies for the american psychologist. meaningful loss in the first six months, regain by year three to five for most. not because people quit. the body lowers its energy use and raises hunger signals to defend the prior weight.
pages.citation: mann et al., american psychologist (2007)

pages.headline: rewrite the [story] of past attempts
pages.body: pick one diet you regained on. write one sentence in the old story. then write one sentence in the new one. old: i fell off. new: the body defended a setpoint and nobody taught me the skills to bridge it. notice which one is true. notice which one your brain wants to keep. that's the script we're rewriting this whole act.

pages.headline: the [skills] are the bridge
pages.body: weight loss alone isn't durable. the skills you learn here are. tomorrow we run a live thought record on whatever your brain is doing with this information.

pages.headline: the [rebound] math, with medicine in the mix
pages.body: you may have already heard the version of this: people regain when they come off glp-1s. that's true, and it's not the whole sentence. medicine shifts the math. it doesn't eliminate it. the skills are what makes the loss a body you can keep living in, whether the medicine stays in the picture or not. okay so today we look at the math, and at where the skills bridge it.

pages.headline: the body [defends] a lower weight
pages.body: mann's review of long-term diet studies showed the body fights back against weight loss through hunger signals and energy use. glp-1s work in part by quieting some of that fight, especially the hunger signal. the studies on stopping the medicine show partial regain when no skills sit underneath it. the medicine is real medicine. the skills are real skills. you build both, you keep both.
pages.citation: mann et al., american psychologist (2007)

pages.headline: name the [skill] under each meal
pages.body: for the next two meals on the medicine, ask one question. what skill is also happening here. paused before eating. checked in with fullness. asked if i was actually hungry. the medicine carries some of the work. the skill carries the rest. both are credited.

pages.headline: the medicine helps. the [skills] keep it.
pages.body: credit the medicine. credit yourself. tomorrow we run a live thought record on whatever today brought up.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: greenberger & padesky, mind over mood (1995/2015)

pages.headline: thought record [live]
pages.body: you've spent two weeks meeting the script, the loop, the body, the math. today we use a tool that puts all of it in five small columns. greenberger and padesky have been teaching it for thirty years. unsexy. it works. pick one thought from the last 48 hours that pulled you down.

pages.headline: the [five] columns
pages.body: mind over mood teaches the move. column one. situation. just the facts. column two. mood. one word, 0 to 100. column three. automatic thought. column four. evidence for and against. column five. balanced thought. the line that holds the truth without the cruelty. then re-rate.
pages.citation: greenberger & padesky, mind over mood (1995/2015)

pages.headline: do [one] record. that's it.
pages.body: open notes. run the five columns on one thought from this week. don't pick the worst one. pick a medium one. you're learning the move, not solving your life. the balanced thought is not a positive thought. it's a true one. that's the only standard.
pages.prompt: what was your balanced thought? write the exact sentence.

pages.headline: the [tool] is portable now
pages.body: you don't have to remember every concept from this act. you just have to remember the five columns. they go with you. tomorrow we close act one and look at what you now actually know.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: what you now [know]
pages.body: three weeks ago you opened this app with a body you were tired of fighting and a voice in your head you thought was your personality. okay so today we look at what's actually different. not pounds. not photos. the things in your head that aren't where they were. that's the whole act one. and that's most of the real work.

pages.headline: the [seven] things that moved
pages.body: you can name food noise as a script. read the scale as weather not climate. spot the all-or-nothing thought before it spirals. call the inner critic a roommate. you know self-compassion predicts less eating. you know the rebound math wasn't your character.
pages.citation: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: name the [one] that moved most
pages.body: of the seven things, which one actually shifted in you. not which one sounded best. which one you caught yourself using this week, even badly. that's your foothold for act two. we don't need all seven solid. we need one real, and the rest can keep softening underneath.
pages.prompt: which one moved most, and what's one sentence of evidence?

pages.headline: act one is the [why]. act two is the how.
pages.body: you've spent three weeks rewiring how you think about food, body, and the voice in your head. tomorrow we start the skills that turn the rewire into a day you can actually live. you don't have to feel like her yet. you just have to move like her once♡

canonical84.workingTitle: tracking is a *mirror*, not a judge
canonical84.evidenceAnchor: burke et al., journal of the academy of nutrition and dietetics (2011)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: no caloric tracking — mood + meal-time only.

pages.headline: tracking is a *mirror*, not a judge
pages.body: it's tuesday. you logged the oat milk latte, the half-bowl of pasta, the handful of almonds at 4. and now you're staring at the app like it's about to *grade* you. tracking only works when it's a mirror. mirrors show what's there. they don't shame.

pages.headline: the *noticing* is the intervention
pages.body: self-monitoring is the most studied behavior in weight research. people who track lose more. not from calories. *noticing* changes choices upstream of the bite. the catch. it only works when the log is non-judgmental.
pages.citation: burke et al., journal of the academy of nutrition and dietetics (2011)

pages.headline: log every bite. judge zero of them.
pages.body: log what you eat today. all of it. the standing-up snacks, the bites off your roommate's plate, the third coffee. log it flat. no commentary, no good/bad column. at the end of the day, look once. notice one pattern. not a verdict. a pattern. that's the whole skill.
pages.data_tie: today's log feeds the trend, not the judge.

pages.headline: you're someone who *looks*. that's it.
pages.body: you don't have to fix what you see. you just have to keep looking. the mirror does the rest.

pages.headline: tracking is a *mirror*, not a judge

pages.headline: the *noticing* is the intervention
pages.body:
self-monitoring works because *noticing* changes choices. but for someone with a restrictive history, calorie tracking is the wrong mirror — it reactivates the rule-brain and the numbers become a courtroom.

the research holds for any structured noticing. you log time and mood. you build the awareness loop without the trigger. same intervention, safer instrument.
pages.citation: burke et al., journal of the academy of nutrition and dietetics (2011)

every time you eat today, jot the clock and one word for how you felt. tired. bored. soft. fine. no food name, no portion, no number.

at the end of the day, read the column once. notice one pattern. that's the whole skill.

pages.headline: you're someone who *looks*. that's it.
pages.body: the mirror you need doesn't have a calorie field. it has a feelings field. you already know how to look. we're just rebuilding the safe version.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: gollwitzer, american psychologist (1999)

pages.headline: *if-then* for the hard moments
pages.body: you know the moment. friday, 6:47, the keys go in the bowl, the bag goes on the chair, and your body walks itself to the freezer before your brain catches up. you've had this conversation four hundred times. willpower keeps showing up to a fight it was never built for.

pages.headline: decide *before* the moment arrives
pages.body: gollwitzer found that if you decide what you'll do in advance, tied to a specific cue, your follow-through roughly doubles. pre-loading. the cue triggers the action. specificity matters. 'when i walk in at 6:45, i pour sparkling water before i open the freezer.'
pages.citation: gollwitzer, american psychologist (1999)

pages.headline: write *one* if-then. say it out loud.
pages.body:
pick the moment you keep losing. one. the 11pm pantry, the post-meeting drawer, the sunday drive past the drive-through. write it like this:

if _______, then i'll _______.

keep the then-move tiny. drink the water. walk to the other room. text one person. it doesn't have to be heroic. it has to be ready.

pages.headline: past-you is *building* the route now
pages.body: you don't have to be stronger at 6:47. you have to be smarter at noon. one if-then, written today, does the carrying.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wansink, mindless eating (2006/2017 reanalysis)

pages.headline: design your *kitchen*, not your willpower
pages.body: look at your counter right now. what's sitting out. the bag of pretzels half-rolled, the cereal box from sunday, the chocolate someone brought from work. and what's hidden. the apples in the bottom drawer, the yogurt behind the leftovers.

pages.headline: your *environment* is louder than your intention
pages.body: wansink's lab found that food on the counter, in clear containers, at eye level, gets eaten, sometimes hundreds of calories a day, without anyone noticing. the food didn't change. the visibility did. stimulus control. you can't fight a cue you see fifty times a day.
pages.citation: wansink, mindless eating (2006/2017 reanalysis)

pages.headline: move *three* things. five minutes total.
pages.body:
walk your kitchen. move three things.

the counter snack goes in an opaque container, behind the cabinet door. one fruit bowl comes out to the counter. one drawer of protein at eye-level in the fridge — string cheese, hard-boiled eggs, whatever you actually eat.

that's it. five minutes. the kitchen now votes with you.

pages.headline: you don't need more willpower. you need a *redesign*.
pages.body: the strong version of you isn't out there negotiating with the pantry. she set the room up so the negotiation never starts.

pages.headline: design your *kitchen*, not your willpower
pages.body: look at your counter right now. or picture it. the pretzels, the cereal, the chocolate from work. and the apples hiding in the drawer. for you, we're not throwing anything away. nothing gets banned, nothing gets purged. the goal isn't a *pure* kitchen. it's a calmer one. we're relocating, not restricting. the difference matters more than you think.

pages.headline: your *environment* is louder than your intention
pages.body:
wansink found that food on the counter, in clear containers, at eye level, gets eaten more — silently. the food didn't change. the visibility did.

for someone with a restrictive history, the move is relocation only. removing a food creates the rule, and the rule creates the urge. but moving the pretzels from the counter to the cabinet doesn't trigger the rule-brain. the food is still *allowed*. it's just not on display.
pages.citation: wansink, mindless eating (2006/2017 reanalysis)

pages.headline: relocate *three* things. nothing leaves.
pages.body:
walk your kitchen. move three things.

the counter snack goes in the cabinet, still fully accessible. one fruit bowl comes out. one drawer of easy protein at eye-level.

nothing gets thrown out. the food stays in the house. you've just turned the volume down on it.

pages.headline: permission stays. the *volume* changes.
pages.body: you're not banning the food. you're choosing how loud it gets to be in the room with you.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: leidy et al., american journal of clinical nutrition (2015)

cohortOverrides.glp1: critical for muscle preservation during weight loss.
cohortOverrides.perimenopause: 1.2-1.6 g/kg target — lean mass is the metabolic currency of the next 30 years.

pages.headline: protein and *satiety*
pages.body: you had the latte and the croissant on the way in and by 10:42 you're at the kitchenette opening the snack drawer, confused, like, you ate three hours ago. not willpower. *satiety*. mostly fast carbs, no protein, the body finishes in ninety minutes and asks for the next thing.

pages.headline: front-load it. the day gets *quieter*.
pages.body: leidy's group ran the trial. women who got around 30g of protein at breakfast reported less afternoon food noise, ate less at dinner, rated themselves fuller into the evening. protein is the most satiating macronutrient. anchor habit. one protein at breakfast. eggs. greek yogurt.
pages.citation: leidy et al., american journal of clinical nutrition (2015)

pages.headline: one *protein*. before the carb.

tomorrow. pick the protein tonight. eggs in the fridge, yogurt in the door, jerky in the bag, whatever you'll actually reach for.

in the morning, the protein lands first. then the coffee, the toast, the rest of your life. you're not changing your breakfast. you're changing the *order*.

pages.headline: you're someone who feeds her *day*, not just her morning
pages.body: the 10:42 drawer-open isn't who you are. it's who you become when breakfast didn't show up for you. tomorrow, breakfast shows up first.

pages.headline: protein and *satiety*
pages.body: the medicine is doing its job. the food noise is quieter. some mornings you forget to eat at all. that's part of why this matters now. when appetite drops and the scale moves, the body doesn't only lose fat — it loses lean tissue too, if you're not feeding the muscle. and that's the part nobody on tiktok mentioned. okay so. protein on glp-1 isn't optional. it's the whole strategy.

pages.headline: muscle is the *currency* of this chapter
pages.body:
leidy's work shows protein at breakfast does the satiety job. on glp-1, satiety is already handled — your job is muscle preservation. clinicians treating glp-1 patients target around 1.2 to 1.6 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight, spread across the day, because that's what protects lean mass during fast loss.

the anchor habit holds. one protein at breakfast, even small. eggs. yogurt. a protein shake if eating feels like a chore. don't wait for hunger — it isn't coming the way it used to.
pages.citation: leidy et al., american journal of clinical nutrition (2015)

pages.headline: one *protein*. even when you're not hungry.
pages.body:
tomorrow. pick the protein tonight, even if appetite is low.

in the morning, it lands first, before the coffee. small portion is fine — a half cup of yogurt counts. the goal isn't fullness. the goal is *showing up* for the muscle that's working for you on the other side of this.

pages.headline: the medicine *helps*. the protein protects.
pages.body: credit the medicine. it's doing real work. and you're doing the work the medicine can't — keeping the body that's going to carry you out of this chapter.

pages.headline: protein and *satiety*
pages.body: the thing nobody told you about this decade is that the protein math just changed on you. what worked at 28 — toast, latte, fine until lunch — stopped working somewhere around now, and you've been quietly wondering if you're broken. okay so. you're not. the receptors are. estrogen decline shifts how the body builds and holds muscle, and the protein floor goes up to meet it. this isn't a willpower problem. it's a *physiology* update.

pages.headline: lean mass is your *next thirty years*
pages.body:
leidy's group showed protein at breakfast wins on satiety. in perimenopause, the bigger story is muscle. peri-research clinicians target 1.2 to 1.6 grams of protein per kilogram body weight, distributed across the day, because lean mass is the single biggest predictor of how the next thirty years feel — strength, metabolism, bone, balance, all of it.

the anchor habit. protein at breakfast, then again at lunch, then at dinner. spread, not stacked. the body absorbs what it can use in the moment.
pages.citation: leidy et al., american journal of clinical nutrition (2015)

pages.headline: one *protein*. before the carb. then again at lunch.
pages.body:
tomorrow. protein lands first at breakfast — eggs, yogurt, cottage cheese, leftover salmon.

at lunch, protein on the plate again. doesn't have to be big. a palm-size portion counts. your body is rebuilding the rules. you're just feeding the rebuild.

pages.headline: you're not behind. you're *recalibrating*.
pages.body: the body changed the rules without sending the memo. you're reading it now. that's the whole work.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: spiegel et al., annals of internal medicine (2004)

cohortOverrides.perimenopause: estrogen decline disrupts sleep architecture — meet yourself where you are, no shame in 5-hour nights.

pages.headline: sleep is *half* your appetite
pages.body: five hours of sleep. you woke up tired, ordered the bigger latte, were starving by 10, snacked through the afternoon meetings, and by 8pm you were eyeing the freezer like it owed you money. you kept asking why today felt so *loud*. it wasn't your character. it was your hormones.

pages.headline: under six hours, the hunger script *changes*
pages.body: spiegel's lab put healthy adults on restricted sleep and measured the hormones. leptin, the fullness signal, dropped. ghrelin, the hunger signal, climbed. cravings tilted toward carbs and sugar. a low-sleep day feels different because it is different.
pages.citation: Update citation to 'tasali et al., jama internal medicine (2022)' or 'st-onge, sleep (2011)' — stronger contemporary real-world citations for sleep-and-appetite than the original ghrelin/leptin paper.

pages.headline: sleep is *half* your appetite
pages.body: 3:14am. you're awake again. you don't even know why. and you already know how tomorrow is going to feel — louder, hungrier, more drawer-opens than you'll be proud of. okay so. this isn't a willpower problem you brought into a new decade. estrogen pulled out of the sleep architecture and didn't leave a forwarding address. meet yourself *here*. no shame in the 5-hour nights. we work with the body that's actually showing up.

the move is not heroic sleep. the move is small protective rituals — cooler room, dimmer light, magnesium if your doctor agrees, and forgiving yourself on the nights it still fragments.
pages.citation: spiegel et al., annals of internal medicine (2004)

pick one: room two degrees cooler, phone out of reach, a single magnesium glycinate with your doc's okay, or just lights down at 9.

one. on the nights it doesn't work, you didn't fail. the body is doing its thing. tomorrow, you'll eat with that information, not against it.

pages.headline: loud days are *weather*, not verdicts

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: brewer et al., drug and alcohol dependence (2011)

pages.headline: the urge *plan*
pages.body: your hand is already on the office snack drawer. it's 2:47, you're not hungry, and a deeply boring spreadsheet is open behind you. the drawer is reliable. the drawer is *kind*, almost. it doesn't ask anything of you. but between the thought and opening, there's a window.

pages.body: brewer's group, from marlatt's relapse prevention model, tracked what happens to an urge when you don't act on it. it rises. peaks. falls. usually inside ninety seconds. the skill isn't fighting it. it's *surfing* it. notice the sensation. let it crest. one tiny substitution.

pick three substitutions you'll actually do. write them down. mine: sparkling water, a two-minute walk to the printer, text my sister something stupid.

when the urge shows up, you don't decide. you reach for the menu. one of the three. ninety seconds. if you still want the snack after, you have it. usually you won't.
