canonical84.evidenceAnchor: segar et al., international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity (2011)

cohortOverrides.low_back_pain: gentle movement only — walks, stretching, low-impact.

pages.headline: movement as *mood*, not punishment
pages.body: you skipped the workout monday. you told yourself you'd make it up tuesday. tuesday you doubled, and hated every second. wednesday you skipped again, with extra guilt. somewhere along the line, movement got drafted as *punishment*.

pages.headline: the why determines the *whether*
pages.body: segar's group followed women across a year. the women who exercised for *immediate* rewards, energy, mood, time outside, feeling like themselves, moved 32 percent more than women who exercised for weight or health. the long-game payoff is too abstract to compete with the couch. when movement becomes how you feel better at 7pm, it stops requiring willpower.
pages.citation: segar et al., international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity (2011)

pages.headline: twelve minutes. for the *mood*.
pages.body:
pick something you'd do even if it changed nothing on the scale. a walk with one good song on. a stretch on the floor while the kettle boils. dancing while you cook.

twelve minutes. the goal isn't calories. the goal is feeling better at 7pm than you did at 6:48. that's the whole metric.
pages.data_tie: the body kept moving on the days the mood paid out, not the days the scale did.

pages.headline: you move *because*, not in order to
pages.body: the body that gets carried forward by feeling-good is the body that keeps showing up. that's the whole experiment.

pages.headline: movement as *mood*, not punishment
pages.body: you skipped the workout monday because your back was talking. tuesday you tried anyway, paid for it. wednesday you weren't sure if rest was wise or weak. okay so. for you, movement-as-punishment is also movement-as-injury. we're undrafting both. for this chapter, the assignment is *gentle*. walks. stretches. low-impact. that's not a downgrade. that's the smart version.

pages.headline: the why determines the *whether*
pages.body:
segar's group found women who moved for mood and energy kept moving 32 percent more than women who moved for weight. for someone with low-back-pain, that finding is doubled — because punishment-framing pushes you toward the intensity that re-injures.

for now, the menu is: walks, gentle stretching, swimming if you have access, mobility flows from your physical therapist. the goal is showing up to a body that doesn't hurt tomorrow.
pages.citation: segar et al., international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity (2011)

pages.headline: twelve minutes. *gentle*. for the mood.
pages.body:
pick one gentle thing. a walk around the block. a five-minute floor stretch. a slow stroll while a podcast plays.

twelve minutes. if anything hurts, you stop. the metric is feeling better at 7pm, with the back still on your side.

pages.headline: the back is *with* you. so are we.
pages.body: moving gently while you heal isn't doing less. it's doing what works. tomorrow your body remembers you were kind.

pages.headline: movement as *mood*, not punishment
pages.body: you skipped the workout monday. and the small voice immediately started doing the math — what you'd eat to make up for it, what you'd cut tomorrow. okay so. for you, this lesson goes deeper than function reframe. movement that's tied to food math reactivates the same loop we're working to quiet. we're going to cap the intensity and drop the numbers, and trust that the *mood* payoff is the whole reason.

pages.headline: the why determines the *whether*
pages.body:
segar's group followed women across a year. the women who moved for energy, mood, and feeling-like-themselves moved 32 percent more than the women who moved for weight or compensation.

for you, the assignment is to never tie movement to what you ate. ever. movement is for how the day feels. that's the only column. cap the intensity. the goal isn't to push. the goal is to enjoy.
pages.citation: segar et al., international journal of behavioral nutrition and physical activity (2011)

pages.headline: twelve minutes. *for joy*. nothing else.
pages.body:
pick something you'd do even if it changed *nothing*. a walk in the sun. dancing in the kitchen. a slow stretch with music.

twelve minutes. the metric is mood at 7pm. no other column.

pages.headline: you move for *you*, not for what you ate
pages.body: the body doesn't owe the day a workout. the day owes the body some joy. that's the trade we're learning♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: linehan, dbt skills training manual (2014)

pages.headline: the *stress* eating chain
pages.body: you ate the whole thing. the chips, the leftover pasta, the spoonful of nutella standing at the counter. and the story afterward was *i have no control*. what happened wasn't one decision. it was a chain. seven links between the boss email at 4:12 and the spoon at 9:47.

pages.headline: the chain has *four* places to break
pages.body: linehan's chain analysis is the gold-standard skill for emotional behavior. you walk the event backward. the cue. the thought. the body. the urge. the action. four interruption points. the cue (boss email), the thought (i'm such a screw-up), the body (tight chest), the action (snack drawer). each is a place to insert a different move. you insert one new link.
pages.citation: linehan, dbt skills training manual (2014)

pages.headline: walk *one* chain backward
pages.body:
pick a recent stress-eat. write the chain. five short lines.

1. what actually happened first (the cue): 
2. the thought that came: 
3. what your body did: 
4. the urge: 
5. the action: 

now circle one link where, next time, you'll try one different move.
pages.prompt: the link i'll try to interrupt next time is _______.

pages.headline: the chain is *visible* now. you can work it.
pages.body: you didn't lose control. you walked a chain you couldn't see yet. today you can see it. that's the whole work.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: balban et al., cell reports medicine (2023)

pages.eyebrow: the only thing you have to do
pages.headline: one *breath* that works
pages.body: you've been told to breathe a hundred times. by yoga teachers, therapists, an instagram graphic. you've nodded politely and not really believed it does anything. there's one breath with actual receipts. stanford ran it against meditation, with real cortisol drops.

pages.headline: the double inhale, the long *exhale*
pages.body: balban's stanford trial compared three breath protocols and mindfulness across a month. cyclic sighing won. it dropped resting heart rate, improved mood, lowered anxiety more than meditation. the mechanism is the long *exhale* activating the parasympathetic side.
pages.citation: Keep citation but add evidence-strength flag in copy: 'an emerging body of work, balban 2023 cell reports (n=114).' Flag as 'emerging' not 'established'.
pages.breath_line: in through the nose. in again on top. long slow out through the mouth. repeat.

pages.headline: *five* sighs. when the day gets loud.
pages.body:
next time the day cranks the volume — the email, the urge, the spiral — five cyclic sighs. wherever you are. the timer in the app counts you through it.

you're not solving the day. you're handing your body a different chemical signal. the loud thing will still be there. you'll be in a different relationship with it.
pages.breath_line: five rounds. inhale, inhale, long exhale.

pages.headline: you carry the *off switch* now
pages.body: you don't need a quiet life to access a quiet nervous system. you just needed the breath that actually works. you have it now♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: forman et al., obesity (2013)

pages.headline: values, not [goals].
pages.body: you're on the train. someone on a podcast asks what your goal weight is. and you notice, maybe for the first time, that the number doesn't land. you've spent five weeks practicing a different question. not what do i weigh by june. what kind of person do i want to be, around food.

pages.headline: goals end. [values] direct.
pages.body: a goal is a destination. you hit it or you don't, and either way it stops working the second you arrive. a value is a direction. care for my body. eat in a way that lets me sleep. forman ran the comparison and the values group held more loss long-term.

pages.body: not goals. directions. write them like you'd say them to a friend. mine usually sound like: i want to eat in a way that lets me think clearly. i want to be in my body, not policing it. i want food to be one of the good parts of a day, not the whole weather report. yours will be different. they should be.
pages.prompt: three values, three sentences. no numbers allowed.

pages.headline: a woman with a [compass].

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: the social [food] situation.
pages.body: it's friday. someone picked the place. the menu is hard to read and the person across from you has already said let's just split a few things. and the old script wakes up. either pre-decide what you're getting and white-knuckle through. or throw the whole week and start monday. neither is who you are anymore. there's a third move.

pages.headline: people who [keep] it use flexibility, not rules.
pages.body: wing and phelan followed thousands who held meaningful weight loss for years. the registry. the thing they share isn't a stricter rule book. it's a flexible one.

pages.headline: the three-line table [script].
pages.body: one. before you order, find the protein on the menu and the thing that actually sounds good. let both be true. two. eat slowly enough to clock fullness. three. when dessert comes, ask if you want it, not if you're allowed it. answer the question you asked. that's the script.

pages.headline: a woman who can [sit] at the table.
pages.body: the script doesn't make food smaller. it makes you steadier in front of it. tomorrow we pull the wider principle out of this one — the 80/20 you actually live.

pages.headline: the social [food] situation.
pages.body: it's friday. someone picked the place. and a familiar feeling is showing up before you've even sat down — the one that wants a plan, a backup plan, and a script for what you'll say if the menu doesn't have your safe order. that part of you has been keeping you safe for a long time. we're not throwing her out. we're just going to give her a little less work tonight, because the principle underneath all of this is flexibility, not a tighter rule.

pages.headline: people who [keep] it use flexibility, not rules.
pages.body: wing and phelan followed thousands of people who lost meaningful weight and held it for years. the line that holds across all of them is flexibility. they eat what's in front of them, they tilt toward protein and fiber when they can, they don't ask any one meal to be perfect. for you, this is the harder skill. the rule-following part feels safer. but the rule is the thing that keeps the binary alive — and you've worked too hard on that binary to feed it tonight.
pages.citation: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: the three-line table [script].
pages.body: one. read the menu like a regular person. find one protein-ish thing and one thing that sounds good. let both exist. two. eat slowly enough to feel full. three. when dessert comes, ask want, not allowed. if your honest answer is no, that's fine. if your honest answer is yes, that's fine. neither answer is a verdict.

pages.headline: a woman who can [sit] at the table.
pages.body: principle is flexibility, not rules. one dinner is not the program. tomorrow we look at what 80/20 actually means in your life.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: westenhoefer et al., appetite (1999)

pages.headline: the [80/20] you actually live.
pages.body: you've seen 80/20 on instagram. usually next to a smoothie bowl and a girl who has never had a loud food day. the implication is a clean column and a sin column. the version that works is quieter. most meals support how you want to feel. a real number simply don't. both count.

pages.headline: flexible eaters [hold] losses. rigid eaters bounce.
pages.body: westenhoefer ran the comparison directly. women who scored high on rigid restraint, the strict-rules, all-or-nothing, forbidden-foods style, regained more weight and reported more binges. women who scored high on flexible restraint, room for a slice of cake on a tuesday without it costing the week, kept the weight off longer.
pages.citation: westenhoefer et al., appetite (1999)

pages.headline: pick the [20] on purpose.
pages.body: look at your week. there are meals you don't get to choose much about. work, people, what's in the fridge. let those be your 80, supported by protein and fiber when possible. then pick the 20 deliberately. the friday dinner. a plan, not a permission slip.

pages.headline: a woman who [chose] her 20.
pages.body: the foods aren't ranked. the choice is. tomorrow we zoom out further — a weekly review you can actually run without dread.

pages.headline: the [80/20] you actually live.
pages.body: the version of 80/20 you've seen has probably felt like permission with a leash on it. 80 perfect, 20 you're nervous about. we're not running that version. we're running the one where you don't have a perfect column at all, because that column was always the engine of the swing. pace this slow. this is the highest-leverage lesson of the week for you, and the goal is not to convince you of flexibility today. just to put it on the table.

pages.headline: flexible eaters [hold] losses. rigid eaters bounce.
pages.body: westenhoefer measured two flavors of restraint. rigid restraint — strict categories, forbidden foods, all-or-nothing — predicted more regain and more binge episodes. flexible restraint — most meals supportive, no foods morally off-limits, no single meal carrying the week — predicted held losses. the data is clear and a little uncomfortable, because rigidity feels safer. you're not weak for finding flexibility hard. you're someone who learned a different operating system, and we're slowly installing a new one.
pages.citation: westenhoefer et al., appetite (1999)

pages.headline: let one [safe] food not be safe.
pages.body: you don't have to pick a 20 yet. today, just look at one food you've been treating as off-limits and let it exist on the same shelf as everything else, in your head. you don't have to eat it. you don't have to not eat it. you're just unranking it. that's the whole assignment.

pages.headline: a woman who [unranked] one thing.
pages.body: small move. real one. tomorrow we use the weekly review to keep the picture wide.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: the [weekly] review.
pages.body: it's sunday. the laundry is half-done, and there's a small impulse to open the app, look at one number from yesterday, and let it decide what kind of week you had. today we interrupt it with a tiny ritual. about three minutes. not a verdict. a debrief. verdicts close the door.

pages.headline: you read the [trend], not the day.
pages.body: the registry people, the ones who keep weight off for years, share one boring habit. they check in regularly, on a schedule, and they read the line through the weeks, not the dot on the morning. one number is weather. four numbers is climate.
pages.citation: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)
pages.data_tie: show 4-week weight trend if user has ≥3 logs; otherwise show skill-practice count.

pages.headline: three lines. no [grading].
pages.body: open your becoming tab. look at the trend line, not yesterday. then write three sentences. one thing that worked. one thing that was harder than i expected. one thing i want to try this week. that's it. no scores, no good-week-bad-week, no resolutions. just data and a next move.
pages.prompt: worked / harder / trying next.

pages.headline: a woman who [reads] the week, not the morning.
pages.body: every sunday gets quieter from here. tomorrow we look at the other end of the day — the morning a maintainer actually runs.

pages.headline: the [weekly] review.
pages.body: sunday has been a hard day for you historically. it's the day the brain tries to make sentences about the week. and most of those sentences have been grades. we're going to do the review differently. no daily number. no good-week / bad-week verdict. just a steady look at the wider line and one honest sentence about what you want to carry into the next seven days.

pages.headline: you read the [trend], not the day.
pages.body: the people who hold weight loss longest check in on a regular cadence, but they read the four-week line, never the morning number. for you specifically, the daily number is not useful — it feeds the part of you that wants to grade. the trend is. it stays quiet, it moves slow, and it doesn't ask anything dramatic of you on any given sunday.
pages.citation: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)
pages.data_tie: show 4-week trend only; suppress daily weight logs in this view.

pages.headline: three lines. no [grading].
pages.body: one thing that worked. one thing that was harder than i expected. one thing i want to try this week. that's the whole review. if you find yourself reaching for a number to make the sentences feel more real, that's the part to notice. the sentences are the data.
pages.prompt: worked / harder / trying next.

pages.headline: a woman who [reads] the week, not the morning.
pages.body: the review is yours. it doesn't grade you. tomorrow we look at the morning.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: lally et al., european journal of social psychology (2010)

pages.headline: the maintainer's [morning].
pages.body: the first hour. you've been collecting evidence about what your first hour actually wants. some water. some protein, eventually. the phone not opened until your feet are on the floor. the maintainer's morning is three things that already work, anchored to things you already do.

pages.headline: anchored habits [settle] into automatic.
pages.body: lally followed people building real habits. on average it took about 66 days for a new behavior to feel automatic, range wide. what made the curve go faster wasn't motivation. it was attaching the new behavior to a cue you already had. after i pour coffee, i drink the water.
pages.citation: lally et al., european journal of social psychology (2010)

pages.headline: anchor [three]. that's the whole morning.
pages.body: pick three small things you want in your first hour. attach each one to something you already do reliably. after my alarm — water. after my coffee — ten minutes of light. after my shower — a protein bite. write it out like a sentence. the structure is the thing the habit grows into. you don't have to feel motivated. the cue carries you.

pages.headline: a woman with a [held] morning.
pages.body: small frame. real keep. tomorrow we move into the harder weather — the feelings list you've been driving around.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: lieberman et al., psychological science (2007)

pages.headline: the hard [feelings] list.
pages.body: it's a wednesday and something at work landed wrong. you're not crying. you're not really anything. you're standing in front of the fridge with the door open and a fog in your chest. the fog doesn't have a name yet. the fridge is offering to give it one.

pages.headline: naming the feeling [softens] the feeling.
pages.body: lieberman put people in scanners and asked them to label what they felt. putting a precise word on it, lonely, anxious, embarrassed, lowered amygdala activity. hard feelings get bigger when you eat past them and smaller when you name them.
pages.citation: lieberman et al., psychological science (2007)

pages.headline: name the [feeling]. then ask what it wants.
pages.body: next time the fridge calls and you're not actually hungry, pause. ask one question. what is this, really. write the word. lonely. tired. understimulated. anxious. then ask what that feeling actually needs. a text to your sister. a nap. a walk. sometimes food. sometimes not.
pages.prompt: fridge call → name → what it wants.

pages.headline: a woman who [names] before she eats.
pages.body: the word is a smaller doorway than the snack. it still gets you out of the fog. tomorrow we look at the most underrated cue — boredom.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: martell et al., behavioral activation for depression (2010)

pages.headline: the [bored] eating fix.
pages.body: it's 3:14. the spreadsheet is open and also somehow closed. you've refreshed the same tab four times. and your hand has done the small migration to the snack drawer twice already, not because you're hungry, just because your nervous system is asking for any signal.

pages.headline: low stimulation pulls you toward [easy] dopamine.
pages.body: martell built behavioral activation for depression and the principle ports cleanly to bored eating. when your activity level drops below a threshold, the brain reaches for the easiest source of stimulation, and food is the most reliable one in most apartments. the fix isn't more discipline. it's a small menu of behaviors available before the snack drawer becomes the answer.
pages.citation: martell et al., behavioral activation for depression (2010)

pages.headline: write the [five-thing] menu.
pages.body: make a list of five things you can do in under ten minutes that ask a little something of your attention. walk to the corner. a face-mask. one chapter. ten minutes of guitar. clean one specific shelf. stick the list somewhere you'll see it at 3pm. the drawer doesn't lose. the drawer just gets one more competitor in the bracket.

pages.headline: a woman with a [menu] for the afternoon.
pages.body: the drawer is still there. the menu is too. tomorrow we move to the harder window — late night.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: garaulet et al., international journal of obesity (2013)

pages.headline: the [late-night] reframe.
pages.body: it's 11pm. the lights are warm. something on screen is finishing. you walk past the kitchen and the kitchen is suddenly very loud. you weren't hungry an hour ago. but the room shifted, and the script that runs in this lighting wakes up.

pages.headline: late calories [hit] different. the room is the cue.
pages.body: garaulet ran a six-month study and found that late eaters lost less, even with the same calories. circadian rhythm changes how the body handles food after dark. not a moral problem. a timing one. the bigger lever is stimulus control. the lighting, the couch.
pages.citation: garaulet et al., international journal of obesity (2013)

pages.headline: close the [kitchen]. open the night.
pages.body: pick a kitchen-closed time. 9. 9:30. whatever fits your life. then build a tiny end-of-day routine that lives outside the kitchen. tea on the bedside. a book in bed. one stretch on the floor. when the 11pm script wakes up, the kitchen is already closed. you rerouted the room.

pages.headline: a woman who [closed] the kitchen.
pages.body: the room held the script. the new room holds the new one. tomorrow we name the three skills that have actually moved the needle for you.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: your top [three] skills.
pages.body: you've collected forty days of evidence about yourself. forty real days. and somewhere in there, three skills have been doing most of the work for you specifically. not the ones the program emphasized. yours. today we pull out the three already yours.

pages.headline: the program belongs to whoever [personalizes] it.
pages.body: cooper, fairburn, and hawker built the cbt-obesity protocol around a quiet idea. long-term keepers are people who turned the general program into a personal one. they identified two or three techniques that worked for their specific brain and made those the spine.
pages.citation: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: name your [three]. write them like vows.
pages.body: look back at the last forty days. which three skills have actually held when it was hard. write them in your own words. mine pauses before the drawer. mine reads the trend, not the morning. mine eats protein first when the day is loud.
pages.prompt: my three skills, in my own words.

pages.headline: a woman with her [own] toolkit.
pages.body: the program shrinks to fit you, not the other way. tomorrow we write the script for the moment the line goes flat.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: müller et al., obesity reviews (2016)

pages.headline: the [plateau] script.
pages.body: the trend went flat the last two weeks. and a familiar story is trying to pick up the pen. you broke it. it stopped working. change something dramatic. that story has run a lot of plateaus into a lot of bounces. the flat part isn't a verdict. it's a phase of the project.

pages.headline: the body [adapts]. plateaus are physiology, not failure.
pages.body: müller reviewed the metabolic adaptation literature. as weight drops, energy expenditure drops with it, hormones shift, and the body lands at a new steady state that can hold for weeks before the line moves again. not willpower failing. homeostasis working. the move the data supports is patience and consistency at the current settings, not a dramatic cut.
pages.citation: müller et al., obesity reviews (2016)
pages.data_tie: show user's 4-week trend with annotation 'flat phases are expected' if slope ≤ 0.1%/wk for ≥10 days.

pages.headline: run the [four-line] plateau script.
pages.body: say it to yourself, out loud if you can. one. the line is flat because the body adapted. two. that means the work is working, not failing. three. i am not going to make a dramatic move in week three of a plateau. four. i am going to keep doing what i was doing and let the line catch up. that's the script. tape it inside your becoming tab.

pages.headline: a woman who [stays] through the flat part.
pages.body: the line will move when the body is ready. tomorrow we close the act on what you now do, in your own voice.

pages.headline: the [plateau] script.
pages.body: the trend went flat. and you can feel an old voice waking up — the one that wants to tighten everything, drop something, prove something. we're not letting that voice write the plateau response. for you specifically, plateaus are the moment the rule-following part of the brain offers you a familiar exit, and that exit has cost you more than it ever helped. we're going to write a different response today. trend-only, no daily number, no dramatic moves.

pages.headline: the body [adapts]. plateaus are physiology, not failure.
pages.body: müller reviewed the metabolic-adaptation literature and the finding is steady. as weight drops, the body lands at new steady states, sometimes for weeks, before the line moves again. cutting harder mostly rebounds. for you, this is also the lesson you've been learning about flexibility, just on a different timescale. the body is doing the same thing the binary brain does — it wants steady, and steady takes time. the work is to not punish it.
pages.citation: müller et al., obesity reviews (2016)
pages.data_tie: show 4-week trend only, no daily entries; annotation 'flat phases are expected' if slope ≤ 0.1%/wk for ≥10 days.

pages.headline: run the [four-line] plateau script.
pages.body: one. the trend is flat because the body adapted. two. the work is working. three. i am not making a dramatic move in week three of a plateau. four. i am keeping the practices i already have. notice if the old voice tries to add a fifth line. that fifth line is the one we're not writing anymore.

pages.headline: a woman who [stays] through the flat part.
pages.body: no dramatic move. no tighter rule. tomorrow we close the act.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: burke et al., journal of the academy of nutrition and dietetics (2011)

pages.headline: what you [now do].
pages.body: six weeks. you didn't think you'd be the kind of person who did six weeks of anything, and here you are. the receipts are quieter. you noticed the voice last tuesday and didn't obey it. you ate the dinner on friday and didn't make it mean anything about saturday.

pages.headline: self-monitoring is the habit that [keeps] all the others.
pages.body: burke ran the largest review of self-monitoring in weight research. people who kept track of any behavior, food, weight, skills, anything consistent, held losses better than people who stopped. paying attention is itself the intervention.
pages.citation: burke et al., journal of the academy of nutrition and dietetics (2011)

pages.headline: write your [six-week] receipts.
pages.body: open your journal. write the sentence: six weeks ago i didn't, and now i do. fill in as many as come honestly. pause before the drawer. read the trend not the morning. name the feeling first. choose the 20 on purpose. close the kitchen. keep going until the list runs out. that list is the spine you walk into act iii with.
pages.prompt: six weeks ago i didn't ___. now i do.

pages.headline: a woman with a [practice], not a program.
pages.body: act ii closes here. act iii is the identity work — who you're being while you do all of this♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: oyserman et al., journal of personality and social psychology (2006)

pages.headline: who would you be in a body you weren't trying to [escape]
pages.body: you've spent years being the friend who's working on it. the one with the food rules. the one who orders the salad and then talks about ordering the salad. it became a personality. and underneath all the working-on-it, there's a question you've been avoiding.

pages.headline: habit follows [identity], not the other way around
pages.body: the cleanest behavior-change finding in the last twenty years. people who say 'i don't' instead of 'i can't' keep the behavior longer. one is a rule you're enforcing on yourself. the other is who you already are. trying becomes exhausting. being is just tuesday.
pages.citation: oyserman et al., journal of personality and social psychology (2006)

pages.headline: finish the sentence. [i am someone who].
pages.body: write it down somewhere only you see it. one line. present tense. not 'someone who's trying to.' not 'someone who wants to be.' just the version of her that's already true on her quietest day. eats when she's hungry. logs the workout. closes the pantry at 11. pick one. that's the seed.
pages.prompt: i am someone who _______.

pages.headline: you don't have to feel like her yet♡
pages.body: you just have to move like her once today. the feeling shows up later, after the evidence stacks. that's the whole order of operations. action first. identity second. the woman she becomes was always going to be you.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: neff & germer, mindful self-compassion workbook (2018)

pages.headline: she's been in your head a [long] time
pages.body: you finish a meal and she starts. should've stopped earlier. shouldn't have ordered that. tomorrow's going to tight. she talks like she's helping. she's the voice you'd never use on your sister, your best friend, the kid you used to be. but she gets the mic.

pages.headline: two chairs. she gets [one]. you get the other.
pages.body: in mindful self-compassion work, the inner critic isn't fought or silenced. she's witnessed. you let her say her piece. then a second voice gets to answer. the one that knows you, has the receipts, and isn't on a deadline. most women have never let that second voice speak. she's been waiting.
pages.citation: neff & germer, mindful self-compassion workbook (2018)

pages.headline: let her talk. then [you] talk.
pages.body: next time she starts, write two columns. left: what she said, word for word. don't soften it. right: what a friend who actually loved you would say to that same sentence. not toxic-positive. just true. you'll feel the difference in your chest, not your head.
pages.prompt: she said: _______ / a friend would say: _______

pages.headline: she's not the enemy. she's just outdated.
pages.body: she learned this voice somewhere, doing her best to keep you safe with the tools she had. today she gets witnessed and corrected, not exiled. tomorrow she gets quieter. that's how this softens.

pages.headline: we're going [slow] here. on purpose.
pages.body: for some of us the inner critic isn't a voice. she's the whole weather. she's been narrating since middle school. today we're not going to confront her. we're going to notice she's there. that's it. one breath of distance. one inch of room between you and the script. that's the work.

pages.headline: two chairs. she gets [one]. you get the other.
pages.body: in mindful self-compassion work, the inner critic isn't fought, fixed, or silenced. she's witnessed. for cohorts with a long history of restriction, the second voice is built slowly. one sentence at a time. if this feels heavy today, close the page and try again later. nothing is owed.
pages.citation: neff & germer, mindful self-compassion workbook (2018)

pages.headline: just [notice] her. don't answer yet.
pages.body: for today, no two-column work. just clock one sentence she said and write it down with no rebuttal. seeing it on paper is the entire skill. the answering voice comes later, when she has more practice and you have more room.
pages.prompt: one thing she said today: _______

pages.headline: the gentlest version of this is the right version.
pages.body: you do not have to fight her to heal. you just have to stop believing her on autopilot. one noticed sentence is the whole win today.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: neff, self and identity (2003)

pages.headline: self-compassion is not soft. it's the [engine].
pages.body: you've been told being hard on yourself is what keeps you in line. that if you stop, you'll spiral. you've run for years on a quiet treadmill of criticism, certain it was the thing holding the program together. it wasn't. it was the thing breaking it.

pages.headline: kind to yourself eats [less], not more
pages.body: in the cleanest experiment on this, women given a self-compassion exercise after eating a donut ate fewer cookies in the next task than women given no instruction. shame predicted more eating. compassion predicted less. criticism feels productive. it isn't.
pages.citation: neff, self and identity (2003)

pages.headline: one sentence. [say it out loud].
pages.body: the next time the critic starts, say this in your actual voice, even if quiet. 'this is a hard moment. lots of women have hard moments. let me be on my own side for ten seconds.' it sounds simple because it is. the brain takes the cue from your own voice faster than from your thinking. ten seconds. that's the dose.

pages.headline: softness is the [performance] move♡
pages.body: you're not slacking when you're kind to yourself. you're tuning the instrument. tomorrow's decisions get made out of this. not the other thing.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: masuda et al., behaviour research and therapy (2004)

pages.headline: a thought showed up. it felt like [truth].
pages.body: you opened the camera. saw your face from an angle you don't usually see. and a sentence dropped in. you know the one. it was specific, it was mean, and it felt completely correct. for about forty seconds you walked around inside it like it was the room. it wasn't the room. it was weather. and today we're going to learn the difference.

pages.headline: the thought isn't the truth. it's a [habit].
pages.body: in cognitive defusion, you stop arguing with the thought and start watching it. the goal isn't to think better thoughts. it's to stop being inside every one of them. when a sentence shows up, try the prefix. 'i'm having the thought that.' suddenly it's a sentence, not a verdict.
pages.citation: masuda et al., behaviour research and therapy (2004)

pages.headline: add the prefix. [watch] what it does.
pages.body: next time a brutal sentence drops in, say it once with the prefix. 'i'm having the thought that my arms look heavy today.' notice the half-inch of room that opens. that room is the whole skill. you don't fight it. you don't fix it. you just stand next to it for a second.

pages.headline: weather, not climate.
pages.body: thoughts move through. they always have. the version of you who notices them isn't the storm. she's the sky. tomorrow keeps showing you that.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cohen et al., body image (2021)

cohortOverrides.postpartum: lead with the year your body just did.

pages.headline: you don't have to [love] her today
pages.body: the wellness industry sold you two doors. hate your body, or love every inch on command. one feels honest and miserable. the other feels performative. there's a third door. neutrality. you can put her down. you don't have to feel anything strong about your thighs at 8am on a tuesday. that's allowed.

pages.headline: neutrality outperforms forced [positivity]
pages.body: in a 2021 study, women practicing body-neutrality reflections reported lower appearance-anxiety than women given body-positivity affirmations. the affirmation group sometimes felt worse.
pages.citation: cohen et al., body image (2021)

pages.headline: name one thing she [did]. not how she looked.
pages.body: walked you up the stairs. carried groceries. held the phone to your ear while you cried last week. one function. write it down or say it out loud. you're moving the camera off the surface and onto the use. that's the move.

pages.headline: she doesn't need your [verdict] today♡
pages.body: she just needs your company. neutral is a real place. you're allowed to live there. tomorrow we keep building it out.

pages.headline: the body in the mirror is not [misbehaving]
pages.body: the shape shifted. the middle holds differently. clothes that fit two years ago don't. that's not failure. that's biology doing exactly what biology does at this hormonal moment. the wellness industry will sell you ten products to fight it. we're going to do something harder and quieter. we're going to stop fighting the body that's been honest with you the whole time.

pages.headline: neutrality outperforms forced [positivity]
pages.body: in a 2021 study, women who practiced body-functionality reflections reported lower appearance-anxiety than women given body-positivity affirmations. the gap between 'i love this new body' and the actual mirror, especially during perimenopause, is sometimes too wide. neutrality is the bridge. your body did not break. it changed. and it still carries you.
pages.citation: cohen et al., body image (2021)

pages.headline: one function. not the [shape].
pages.body: her legs got you through the day. her hands texted your kid back. her chest holds your breath when your jaw tightens at 3pm. name one function. that's where the camera goes. shape can keep changing. usefulness is steady.

pages.headline: she's not the body you had. she's the body you have♡
pages.body: and the body you have is the one doing the work. neutrality means you stop spending the day grieving the other one. tomorrow keeps building from here.

pages.headline: look at what she just [carried]
pages.body: she grew a person. she fed a person, or tried to. she ran on broken sleep for months. she went to appointments. she healed tissue you can't even see. and somewhere in the middle of all that, the internet asked her to snap back. there is no snapping back. there's only forward. and forward starts with putting down the comparison to who she was before.

pages.headline: neutrality outperforms forced [positivity]
pages.body: in a 2021 study, women who practiced body-functionality reflections reported lower appearance-anxiety than women given body-positivity affirmations. for postpartum bodies, that gap is wider than for almost any other moment in a woman's life. neutrality says: this body is currently doing something extraordinary. it does not need your aesthetic verdict to be enough.
pages.citation: cohen et al., body image (2021)

pages.headline: one thing she [did]. nothing she looked like.
pages.body: lifted the baby. healed a stitch. carried the diaper bag and a coffee and a toddler. write one function down. the camera does not go on her shape today. her shape is allowed to be wherever it is.

pages.headline: she's a body in the middle of [becoming]♡
pages.body: you don't take a verdict on a body mid-recovery. you let her finish. tomorrow she gets to keep finishing.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes et al., acceptance and commitment therapy (1999)

pages.headline: there's a part of you that's been [watching] the whole time
pages.body: you've had a hundred thoughts today. some kind, some brutal, most boring. you've felt hungry, full, anxious, fine. all of it moved through. but something stayed. the same something that's been in the room since you were six. she is not the rules. she is not the thoughts.

pages.headline: you are the [sky]. the thoughts are the weather.
pages.body: in ACT this is called self-as-context. the self that has the thoughts, instead of the self that is the thoughts. if you are the thought 'i blew it tonight,' the day is over. if you are the place that thought passed through, you put the kettle on and try again at breakfast.
pages.citation: hayes et al., acceptance and commitment therapy (1999)

pages.headline: find the [watcher]. say hi.
pages.body: close your eyes for one breath. notice you're thinking. notice you can notice. that noticer is older than every food rule you've ever learned. she's the part of you that isn't broken because she was never the thing being criticized. when the day gets loud, drop back into her seat for five seconds.

pages.headline: she's the part that's been [you] the whole time
pages.body: everything else moves. she stays. that's where the work is steady from. tomorrow we build out from that seat.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: forman & butryn, the mind your body program (2016)

pages.headline: what does the [smaller] body get you
pages.body: you've said the number. you've pictured the dress. but underneath the goal weight there's another thing you actually want, and most women never say it out loud. to walk into the room without doing math. to take a picture without flinching. to stop being late to your own life.

pages.headline: [values], not goals, run the maintainer
pages.body: in acceptance-based weight programs, the women who maintain for years aren't the ones with the most ambitious goal weights. they're the ones who articulated what kind of life they were doing this for. goals are finite. values are infinite.
pages.citation: forman & butryn, the mind your body program (2016)

pages.headline: write past the [number].
pages.body: finish three sentences in one sitting. don't edit. if the goal weight happened, i'd finally. the version of me that already lives this way spends her tuesday. when i'm ninety, the part of this i'll be glad i did is. read them back tomorrow. those are your real coordinates.
pages.prompt: i'd finally _______ / her tuesday looks like _______ / at ninety i'll be glad i _______

pages.headline: the goal points. the value [carries].
pages.body: the number gets you on the path. the answer to those three sentences keeps you on it. tomorrow we start using it as the compass.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: tiggemann & slater, international journal of eating disorders (2014)

pages.headline: the room you're in is shaping the [body] you want
pages.body: you scrolled for nine minutes this morning. by the end you wanted a different jawline, a different waist, and a different apartment. none of those wants were yours when you woke up. the room you spend time in is not neutral. it's a mirror that bends. and the bend determines what you think you're missing. today we audit the room.

pages.headline: feeds train [comparison] the way gyms train muscle
pages.body: in a 2014 study, the more time adolescent girls spent on appearance-focused social media, the higher their body-surveillance scores. dose response. you are not weaker than the algorithm. the algorithm is well-funded and personalized to your last flinch.
pages.citation: tiggemann & slater, international journal of eating disorders (2014)

pages.headline: mute three. follow [two].
pages.body: mute the three accounts that always leave you feeling smaller. follow two that make you feel like a whole person with a life and not just a body. don't unfollow anyone you'd have to explain to. quiet is enough. the feed is a room you decorate.

pages.headline: the room you stand in [shapes] what you reach for
pages.body: you don't have to leave the internet. you have to curate the part that touches your morning. tomorrow you wake up in a slightly better room.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: fardouly & vartanian, current opinion in psychology (2016)

pages.headline: you saw her arms and your whole day [bent]
pages.body: she was on your feed for four seconds. arms you don't have. a back angle that doesn't happen in real bodies without lighting. you closed the app. but the day already bent. the lunch you were excited about now felt suspicious. the plan you made felt soft. four seconds. that's the snare. and it's been catching you for a long time without a name.

pages.headline: upward comparison [drops] mood and food choice quality
pages.body: in a 2016 review, social comparison on image-based feeds lowered mood and body satisfaction in young women, especially upward comparisons with edited images. the snare isn't that you compared. it's forgetting the math isn't fair.
pages.citation: fardouly & vartanian, current opinion in psychology (2016)

pages.headline: name the [math]. break the spell.
pages.body: next time it happens, say one sentence in your head. 'i'm comparing my whole life to her one frame.' that's it. you don't have to like the photo or hate it. you just have to remember what you're looking at. the snare loosens the second you name it.

pages.headline: her best frame is not your full [life]
pages.body: you have something she'll never have access to. the inside of yours. that's where the win is built. tomorrow keeps building it.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: forman et al., behavior therapy (2013)

pages.headline: the feeling isn't the [problem]. running from it is.
pages.body: you've spent years organizing your eating around feelings you didn't want to feel. boredom at 3pm. loneliness at 10. the small grief that shows up after a good day for reasons nobody warned you about. food has been the answer because food works. for ninety seconds.

pages.headline: willing women keep the [weight] off longer
pages.body: in acceptance-based treatment, women who got better at willingness, sitting with hard internal experiences instead of acting to remove them, kept weight off longer. willingness isn't liking the feeling. it's saying, this is here, i don't have to fix it in ten seconds with a food.
pages.citation: forman et al., behavior therapy (2013)
pages.breath_line: one cyclic sigh: long in through the nose, second short in, long slow out.

pages.headline: one breath. one [sentence]. then choose.
pages.body: next time you're at the cabinet not-hungry, take one cyclic sigh. then say, 'i'm willing to feel whatever this is for the next 90 seconds.' set a timer if it helps. at the end, decide. food might still be the answer. it also might not. you'll know because you waited.
pages.breath_line: in through the nose twice. long out through the mouth.

pages.headline: willing isn't [wanting]. it's allowed.
pages.body: you don't have to want the feeling. you just have to let it sit in the room for a minute. that minute is where the new choice lives.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: alleva et al., journal of consulting and clinical psychology (2015)

cohortOverrides.postpartum: neutral language only, no comparison to pre-pregnancy.

pages.headline: you walk past her [twenty] times a day
pages.body: the full length one by the door. the bathroom one. the dim hallway one you actually like. you've spent years either avoiding her or interrogating her. neither move ever left you better. there's a third version that's well-studied. it stops asking the mirror to lie or to verdict.

pages.headline: looking on [purpose] beats avoiding and beats interrogating
pages.body: in graded mirror exposure, women who looked at their reflection on purpose, for a set time, describing what they saw in neutral language, reported lower body dissatisfaction weeks later. neutral language is the active ingredient. shoulder, not bad shoulder.
pages.citation: Update citation to 'delinsky & wilson, international journal of eating disorders (2006); vocks et al., body image (2008) meta-analysis' — Alleva 2015 was a body-functionality RCT, not a mirror exposure trial.

pages.headline: two minutes. [neutral] words only.
pages.body: stand in front of the mirror you usually rush past. set a two-minute timer. describe what you see, like a witness, not a judge. shoulder. collarbone. waistband. softness. no adjective with a vote. when the timer ends, walk away. no verdict.

pages.headline: the mirror stops being a [courtroom]♡
pages.body: it becomes a window you can pass without flinching. that's all this exercise is asking. tomorrow the next look is a little less loaded.

pages.headline: this one needs a [clinician] in the room with you
pages.body: mirror exposure is a real treatment with real benefits, and for women with a history of restriction or an eating disorder it's done with a therapist trained in it. not solo, not on a tuesday, not from a phone. today we skip the exposure and do the conceptual version instead. you'll get the gist. and you'll keep yourself safe, which is the whole point of every lesson in here.

pages.headline: the technique [works], with the right support
pages.body: in clinical settings, women who did graded mirror exposure with a trained therapist reported lower body dissatisfaction and lower checking behaviors weeks later. the active ingredient is neutral language under clinical guidance. without that guidance the exposure can re-cue checking and shame loops for some cohorts. this lesson exists so you know the tool is real. it does not exist as instruction to run it tonight.
pages.citation: alleva et al., journal of consulting and clinical psychology (2015)

pages.headline: skip the exposure. [tell] someone.
pages.body: if mirror work is on your radar, that's a conversation for your therapist or a clinician trained in body-image exposure. today's skill is naming that out loud to yourself or to someone. no mirror reps. no homework. you knowing the technique exists is enough for the lesson to count.

pages.headline: the gentlest path is still the [path]♡
pages.body: skipping this rep is not skipping the work. it's doing the work intelligently. tomorrow picks back up where you are.

pages.headline: the mirror gets [neutral] words, nothing else
pages.body: your body has spent the last year doing more than most bodies ever will. the mirror you used to know is a different mirror now. today's version of this exercise has one absolute rule. no comparison to the body before. she did her thing. this one is doing hers. we're going to learn how to look at her without filing a report.

pages.headline: looking on [purpose] beats avoiding and beats interrogating
pages.body: in graded mirror exposure work, women who described what they saw in neutral language reported lower body dissatisfaction weeks later. for postpartum bodies the language gets even stricter. nothing about then-versus-now. no pre-pregnancy reference. shoulder, neck, waistband. the mirror becomes a witness for the body that's actually in the room.
pages.citation: alleva et al., journal of consulting and clinical psychology (2015)

pages.headline: two minutes. [present] tense only.
pages.body: set a two minute timer in front of the mirror. describe what you see in present-tense neutral nouns. shoulder. softness. waistband. no comparison to a previous body. no adjective with a verdict. when the timer ends, walk away. that's the rep.

pages.headline: this body is the [one] doing the work♡
pages.body: she does not need a comparison to anybody, including her own past. neutral. present. enough. tomorrow keeps building from her.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes et al., act practitioner's guide (2011)

pages.headline: two things you love are pulling [different] ways
pages.body: it's friday. your friend wants pasta at the place with the good light. you want the pasta. you also want to feel quiet in your body tomorrow. and inside your chest there is a small tight thing that wants both, and is mad at itself for wanting both.

pages.headline: the goal isn't [picking]. it's holding both.
pages.body: values clarification in ACT isn't a ranking. it's a compass with more than one needle. when two values pull apart, the work isn't to amputate one. it's to ask what each one wants from you this week. closeness might want the dinner. body-care might want a slow sunday morning.
pages.citation: hayes et al., act practitioner's guide (2011)

pages.headline: name the two. then ask what each one [actually] wants.
pages.body: pick one decision this week where you feel pulled. write the two values out loud. closeness and body-care. ambition and rest. spontaneity and steadiness. then ask each one, separately, what it needs from you this week. not this hour. this week.
pages.prompt: the two values pulling at me this week are ___ and ___. what each one needs from me is ___.

pages.headline: she doesn't [choose] between her loves. she holds them.
pages.body: we're not picking sides anymore. we're learning to carry two true things at the same time. that's the whole adult skill♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: alleva & tylka, body image (2021)

cohortOverrides.low_back_pain: avoid capable-body framing — lead with personalization.

pages.headline: your body has been [doing] things while you criticized it
pages.body: you woke up this morning. your heart did about a hundred thousand beats yesterday without asking. your lungs handled the stairs. your hands typed the message to your sister. and somewhere in the middle of all that quiet work, you caught the mirror and the script started up again.

pages.headline: the body image we keep is the one we [practice]
pages.body: alleva and tylka's work on functional body image shows that when women redirect attention from how the body looks to what it does, moves, senses, creates, connects, body satisfaction goes up and disordered eating goes down. not because the body changed. because the lens did.
pages.citation: alleva & tylka, body image (2021)

pages.headline: name three things your body [did] for you this week
pages.body: not three things it looks like. three things it did. carried groceries. laughed at the right thing. slept through a hard night. held someone. write them down. small ones count more than impressive ones.
pages.prompt: three things my body did for me this week: ___. ___. ___.

pages.headline: she [thanks] the body for the work it's already doing
pages.body: we're not earning her trust. we're returning it. the body never left♡

pages.headline: your body has been [carrying] something most people don't see
pages.body: you woke up and the back said hello before you did. it's been the loudest thing in the room for a while. and underneath the ache, very quietly, your body has been doing other things. it walked you to the kettle. it held you upright through a meeting. it took breaths you didn't have to plan. some days it does less than you want. some days it does more than you expected. both are true. and neither one says anything about who you are.

pages.headline: the body image we keep is the one we [practice]
pages.body: alleva and tylka's work on functional body image shows that redirecting attention to what the body does — moves, senses, connects, endures — improves body satisfaction across populations, including women managing chronic pain. the practice doesn't require the body to be capable in the gym-bro sense. it asks you to notice what it carried today, even when carrying was the work.
pages.citation: alleva & tylka, body image (2021)

pages.headline: name three things your body [got] you through this week
pages.body: not capable things. carried things. a morning. a conversation. a meal you didn't expect to feel at. write them down on the days the back is loud. these still count. they count more.
pages.prompt: three things my body got me through this week: ___. ___. ___.

pages.headline: she [honors] the body that is showing up tired
pages.body: we're not grading the body for performance. we're keeping it company in the work♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes et al., act 2nd ed (2011)

pages.headline: the rule was strict. was it [working]?
pages.body: you had a rule. no eating after 8pm. it sounded clean. you held it most nights and broke it on a thursday and felt like a person who breaks rules. the rule was strict. but did it work? did it move you toward the life you actually want, or toward shame and a 10pm pantry standoff?

pages.headline: ACT doesn't ask if it's [true]. it asks if it works.
pages.body: the workability test is one of the cleanest moves in ACT. you take a thought, a rule, a strategy, and instead of debating whether it's correct, you ask whether holding it moves you toward your values or away.
pages.citation: hayes et al., act 2nd ed (2011)

pages.headline: pick one rule. ask if it's [working].
pages.body: name one food rule you still carry. no carbs at night. no eating off the plan. no second helping. ask the question out loud: is holding this moving me toward the life i want, or away from it? answer honestly. it doesn't have to be retired today. it just has to be audited.

pages.headline: she keeps what [works]. she lets the rest go.
pages.body: we're not collecting rules anymore. we're keeping a small, useful kit♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes & smith, get out of your mind (2005)

pages.headline: the voice is a [passenger]. you're still driving.
pages.body: imagine you're driving a bus. your bus. you know where it's going. and over the years, passengers got on. the voice that calls foods bad. the voice that says you'll never stick to anything. they shout.

pages.headline: you don't need to evict them. you need to keep [driving].
pages.body: hayes and smith's passengers-on-the-bus metaphor is one of the gentlest moves in ACT. you stop fighting your thoughts as enemies and start treating them as backseat noise. they can shout. they don't get the wheel. the wheel is values. the wheel is the next exit.
pages.citation: hayes & smith, get out of your mind (2005)

pages.headline: next time the voice shouts, say [thanks] and keep going
pages.body: when the script pipes up — about a meal, a mirror, a number — try this. say thanks, voice. noted. and then keep doing what you were doing. the meal. the morning. the walk. you don't have to argue. you just have to keep your hand on the wheel.

pages.headline: she lets them ride. she doesn't let them [drive].
pages.body: we're not exiling parts of ourselves anymore. we're choosing the road♡

canonical84.workingTitle: perfectionism is the diet brain in a suit
canonical84.evidenceAnchor: shafran et al., clinical perfectionism (2010)

pages.headline: it stopped calling itself a [diet]. it called itself standards.
pages.body: you stopped doing diets. you said it out loud at brunch. and then somewhere quietly, the same voice put on a blazer and called itself something nicer. high standards. attention to detail. it tracked the macros under a different app. made one missed walk feel like a verdict.

pages.headline: the rule isn't [high]. it's punishing.
pages.body: shafran's group distinguishes clinical perfectionism from healthy striving by one feature. self-worth becomes contingent on meeting the standard. the cost shows up when you miss it. collapse, restriction, self-attack. notice the rule. soften the all-or-nothing wording.
pages.citation: shafran et al., clinical perfectionism (2010)

pages.headline: find one standard. add the word [usually].
pages.body: pick one rule that's been doing perfectionism work in disguise. i never skip the morning walk. i always log every meal. add the word usually. say it out loud. notice what your body does when the rule softens by one word. that's the size of the change we need.

pages.headline: she has [standards]. she doesn't have a tribunal.
pages.body: we're not lowering the bar. we're letting the bar be a bar, not a verdict♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: beck, cognitive therapy: basics and beyond (2011)

pages.headline: under every food thought, there's [one] sentence
pages.body: it's sunday. you ate the pasta on friday. and the thought today is i have no control around carbs. it sounds like a fact. if that's true, what does it mean? i can't be trusted with food. and if that's true? i'll never be the person i want to be.

pages.headline: the surface thought is the [door]. the belief is the room.
pages.body: beck's downward arrow walks a thought down to its core belief by asking, if that were true, what would it mean? a few times. the belief at the bottom is almost always one painful sentence. i'm not enough. i'm too much. i'm unlovable as i am. it's old code in your own voice.
pages.citation: beck, cognitive therapy: basics and beyond (2011)

pages.headline: walk one thought down. then write the [kinder] true thing.
pages.body: take one food thought from this week. ask the downward arrow question three times. land on the core belief. then write one sentence next to it that's both kinder and true. not a slogan. a sentence you'd believe at the kitchen table.
pages.prompt: the surface thought was ___. underneath, the belief was ___. the kinder true sentence i'm carrying instead is ___.

pages.headline: she changed [one] sentence. and the room moved.
pages.body: we don't rewrite every belief today. we rewrite the one that's been carrying the most weight. that's the whole wedge♡

pages.headline: under every food rule, there's [one] quiet sentence
pages.body: it's sunday. you ate dinner with friends and the meal was bigger than the rule allowed. and the thought today is i'm out of control. we'll move slowly with this one, because i know it lands hard. so just gently — if that thought were true, what would it mean about you? and underneath that, what would that mean? somewhere down there is a sentence you've been carrying for a long time. we're not going to wrestle it. we're going to look at it in good light, with company.

pages.headline: the surface thought is the [door]. the belief is the room.
pages.body: beck's downward arrow technique gently walks a thought down to its core belief by asking, if that were true, what would it mean? we go slowly here, especially when food has carried a lot. the belief at the bottom is almost always one painful sentence about self. it isn't truth. it's old code spoken in your own voice. we don't have to break it today. we just have to see it clearly and put a kinder, accurate sentence next to it.
pages.citation: beck, cognitive therapy: basics and beyond (2011)

pages.headline: walk one thought down. softly. write the [kinder] true thing.
pages.body: take one food thought from this week. ask the downward arrow question two or three times — stop whenever it feels like enough. write the belief you found. then write one sentence next to it that's both kinder and true. if today feels like too much, just notice the surface thought. that's enough.
pages.prompt: the surface thought was ___. underneath, what i noticed was ___. the kinder true sentence i'm carrying instead is ___.

pages.headline: she met [one] sentence with kindness. that's the work.
pages.body: we move at the pace your body trusts. one sentence, looked at gently, is plenty for today♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: tylka & wood-barcalow, body image (2015)

pages.headline: it's not [love] or hate. there's a whole hallway between.
pages.body: the internet sold you two doors. body positivity, where you adore the body at every angle forever. and body shame, where you don't. most days you're in the hallway between them feeling like you failed. you didn't. the hallway is where actual humans live. neutral. respectful.

pages.headline: the healthy version isn't [adoration]. it's appreciation.
pages.body: tylka and wood-barcalow define positive body image as appreciation, acceptance, attentiveness to needs, protective filtering of media. not constant love. it includes neutral days. it includes hard days. the love-or-hate dichotomy is part of the problem.
pages.citation: tylka & wood-barcalow, body image (2015)

pages.headline: name where you are on the [hallway] today
pages.body: check in once today. not the love-or-hate question. the where-am-i-on-the-spectrum question. neutral, respectful, grateful, tender, tired, annoyed but kind. say it in your own words. notice that it isn't binary. notice that you're allowed to move tomorrow.

pages.headline: she lives in the [middle]. she's not failing the assignment.
pages.body: we're not chasing adoration. we're keeping company with the body, room by room♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hershfield et al., journal of marketing research (2011)

pages.headline: she's not a [stranger]. she's you, a year out.
pages.body: imagine her for a second. you, a year from now. she wakes up in your apartment. she drinks the coffee the way you drink it. she still texts your sister. she also has nine more months of this work in her. she has gotten gentler with herself. she's not a stranger.

pages.headline: the more real she feels, the more you act [for] her
pages.body: hershfield's research on future-self continuity shows that when people experience their future self as a vivid, connected version rather than a stranger, they make more long-horizon decisions in that self's favor. the move is psychological.
pages.citation: hershfield et al., journal of marketing research (2011)

pages.headline: write her a [short] letter. or write the one she'd write you.
pages.body: pick a direction. a letter to her — what you want her to know, what you're trying to build for her, what you're sorry about, what you're proud of starting. or a letter from her — what she'd tell you about this week, what she's grateful you did, what she wants you to soften about.
pages.prompt: dear future me, what i'm building for you this week is ___. what i want you to know is ___.

pages.headline: she's already [voting] for the woman she's becoming
pages.body: every small choice today is a postcard sent forward. she'll get them. she's already reading♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: forman & butryn, the mind your body program (2016)

pages.headline: you don't [feel] like her yet. move like her once.
pages.body: you're in the kitchen at 9pm. she, the future one, would probably make a small plate and sit down with it. you don't quite feel like her yet. the feeling lags. the feeling is the last thing to update. so we don't wait. you make the plate. you sit. you eat without scrolling.

pages.headline: in ACT, you act [toward] values before the feeling arrives
pages.body: forman and butryn's acceptance-based treatment uses committed action as the engine. you choose a values-aligned behavior and do it whether or not motivation has shown up. the feeling almost never leads. behavior leads, identity follows, feeling catches up last.
pages.citation: forman & butryn, the mind your body program (2016)

pages.headline: pick one thing she'd do. do that [one] thing.
pages.body: name one small thing the future-you would do today. sit down for the meal. text the friend back. take the walk before the inbox. it doesn't have to be impressive. it has to be hers. do it once. log it quietly in your own head as a vote.

pages.headline: she [moves] like her on the days she doesn't feel like her
pages.body: we're not waiting to feel ready. we're casting votes♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: oyserman et al., journal of personality and social psychology (2006)

pages.headline: the woman who started this isn't [quite] in the room anymore
pages.body: think back nine weeks. the tightness in your chest about food. how you talked about your body in the mirror. the 11pm pantry standoff. and somewhere between then and now, on tuesdays nobody noticed, you started doing things differently. you paused before the drawer. you logged the up-day and kept going. she's not gone. she's just not the only person in the room.

pages.headline: the identity that [holds] is the one you've practiced into
pages.body: oyserman's identity-based motivation work shows that identities become durable through repeated congruent action, not declaration. you don't become the woman you want to be by deciding it. you become her by stacking small choices that look like her until your sense of self catches up. nine weeks have been that stacking. they aren't lessons. they're who you are.
pages.citation: oyserman et al., journal of personality and social psychology (2006)

pages.headline: write the three things you do now that you [didn't] then
pages.body: not pounds. not numbers. behaviors. ways of speaking to yourself. small choices that used to be hard. write three. tiny ones count. tiny ones are the proof.
pages.prompt: three things i do now that i didn't nine weeks ago: ___. ___. ___.

pages.headline: the program isn't [ending]. it's becoming the way you live.
pages.body: from tomorrow we're in maintenance — not the after-part, the actual part. same skills. lower volume. real life. she's ready. so are we♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: maintenance *is* the program
pages.body: you woke up today and the work didn't end. there's no finish line at week twelve, no after-photo, no version of you who graduates out of having a body. the women who keep the weight off don't stop doing the things. they just do them quieter. with less performance.

pages.headline: we treat this like *weather*, not a project
pages.body: the registry has tracked long-term maintainers for over thirty years. the headline finding is unsexy. people who keep weight off treat it as ongoing management, not a phase they completed. closer to dental care than to a season of a show. you floss because that's how teeth stay.
pages.citation: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: rename it from project to *practice*
pages.body:
for the next twenty-one days we're not learning new tricks. we're tuning what already works for the long haul. notice when your brain reaches for 'when i'm done' language. catch it. swap in 'how i live now.' tiny word, different orientation.

you're not finishing. you're settling in.

pages.headline: she's not on a program anymore. she's just *her*.
pages.body: today, when you do the small things, do them like a maintainer would. no big feeling required. tomorrow we talk about what to do the day it goes sideways.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: marlatt & donovan, relapse prevention (2005)

pages.headline: lapse is not *relapse*
pages.body: saturday around 4pm. the wedding food was wedding food, the wine kept arriving, and on sunday morning you stood in the kitchen and felt the old script load. well, that's it then. might as well finish what's in the pantry. start over monday. she's loud and she's wrong.

pages.headline: one slip is data. a *spiral* is a story.
pages.body: marlatt spent his career studying how people recover from a behavioral slip. a lapse is one event. a relapse is the meaning your brain assigns that turns it into a full return. the lapse itself rarely undoes progress. the interrupt point is between the event and the story.
pages.citation: marlatt & donovan, relapse prevention (2005)

pages.headline: name the last lapse out loud and *stop* the story
pages.body: write down the last off-plan moment you had. one sentence. then write the story your brain tried to attach to it. notice they're two separate things. the first is a fact. the second is a forecast you can decline.
pages.prompt: the event was: ___. the story i almost believed was: ___.

pages.headline: she has off days. she doesn't have off *weeks*.
pages.body: today, the next bite is just the next bite. it doesn't carry yesterday with it. tomorrow resets. they all do.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: the maintainer's *daily three*
pages.body: monday. the meeting ran long, the dog had opinions about the rain, and by 9pm you had done none of the things you do on a good day. and yet. you ate when you usually eat. you moved a little. you stepped on the scale tuesday morning anyway.

pages.headline: three behaviors do most of the *holding*
pages.body: the national weight control registry has tracked over ten thousand long-term maintainers. when researchers boil down the shared behaviors, three keep showing up. a consistent eating pattern. some regular movement, often modest. and self-monitoring, usually weekly weighing. notice what's not on the list. perfect macros. heroic workouts. tracking every bite forever.
pages.citation: wing & phelan, american journal of clinical nutrition (2005)

pages.headline: write your *daily three*
pages.body: pick three behaviors so small you could do them on your worst day. one food-related, one movement, one check-in. these are your floor. above them is bonus. below them is the rebuild moment. keep them where you can see them.
pages.prompt: my daily three: 1) ___ 2) ___ 3) ___.

pages.headline: she has a floor. that's the whole *trick*.
pages.body: today, just hit the three. doesn't matter if nothing else happens. tomorrow we map the rooms where the floor gets tested.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: marlatt & gordon, relapse prevention (1985)

pages.headline: high-risk situations *mapped*
pages.body: you can probably name them already. the sunday night kitchen after a long weekend. the work trip with the catered everything. your mother's house. that one friend's birthday where it always goes the same way. these aren't moral failures. they're patterns.

pages.headline: the situations are *predictable*. so the plans can be.
pages.body: marlatt and gordon's relapse prevention work found that about three quarters of slips happen in a small set of recurring situations. negative emotional states. interpersonal conflict. social pressure. positive states where the guard goes down. once a situation is named in advance, it stops being an ambush.
pages.citation: marlatt & gordon, relapse prevention (1985)

pages.headline: list your *top five*
pages.body: name the five situations where things most often go sideways. for each, write one sentence of plan. not a vow. a plan. who you'll text, what you'll order first, when you'll leave. small specific moves you can run on autopilot.
pages.prompt: five high-risk rooms: ___. for each, my pre-decided move: ___.

pages.headline: she walks in *prepared*. not anxious.
pages.body: today, keep the list somewhere you'll see it. that's the whole skill. tomorrow we talk about the version of you who changes with the seasons.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: ma et al., obesity (2006)

cohortOverrides.perimenopause: cycle predictability shifts — new pattern, not failure.

pages.headline: *seasonal* you
pages.body: the light shifts in late october and your body knows before your calendar does. you want soup. you want bed by 9. the salad you ate every day in july suddenly reads like punishment. the script tries to make this mean you're slipping.

pages.headline: the *body* runs on seasons. let the plan do the same.
pages.body: research on seasonal weight patterns shows most people gain a small amount in fall and winter and lose it in spring. mild and predictable. not a failure. a feature of being human in a climate. the maintainer doesn't fight the season. she anticipates it.
pages.citation: ma et al., obesity (2006)

pages.headline: pre-write the *next* season
pages.body: look at what's coming in the next ninety days. weather, calendar, energy. write down one food shift, one movement shift, one rhythm shift that fits the season you're moving into. you're not pivoting. you're translating.

pages.headline: she changes with the weather. the *climate* holds.
pages.body: today, give yourself permission to want what the season wants. tomorrow we plan for the loudest one.

pages.headline: *seasonal* you
pages.body: the light shifts and your body is already running a different program than it was last year. the rhythm that used to be a monthly pattern has gotten harder to read. sleep is its own season now. some weeks the leggings fit differently and the script tries to call it slipping. but this isn't slipping. this is a new pattern, not a failure. and your plan gets to learn the new pattern instead of pretending the old one still applies.

pages.headline: the *body* runs on seasons. let the plan do the same.
pages.body:
research on seasonal weight patterns shows small predictable shifts across the year for most people. in perimenopause the shifts on top of that get louder. cycle predictability changes. sleep changes. appetite changes. none of this is you doing it wrong.

the maintainer doesn't fight the new pattern. she watches for it. names it. translates her daily three into something that fits the body she actually has this season.

cycle predictability shifts. new pattern, not failure.
pages.citation: ma et al., obesity (2006)

pages.headline: pre-write the *next* season
pages.body: look at the next ninety days with the body you have now, not the one you had two years ago. one food shift, one movement shift, one sleep-protective shift. you're translating the plan into your current biology.

pages.headline: she changes with the weather. the *climate* holds.
pages.body: today, listen to the body in front of you. tomorrow we plan for the loudest season.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: yanovski et al., new england journal of medicine (2000)

pages.headline: the *holiday* plan

pages.headline: the plan gets *rehearsed*, not performed
pages.body: yanovski's team studied real eating across the american holiday season. the finding most people miss. holiday weight gain is mostly small and mostly doesn't come off in january. the gain happens in six weeks of unrehearsed eating, not on the actual holidays. behavioral rehearsal is the move. walk through the event. who you sit next to. what hits the plate first.

pages.headline: rehearse the next *big* one
pages.body: pick the next gathering. write three sentences. arrival move. plate move. exit move. you're not deciding what you'll eat. you're deciding how you'll show up. the food gets to be the food.

pages.headline: the *holiday* plan

pages.headline: the plan gets *rehearsed*, not performed

yanovski and colleagues studied actual eating patterns across the american holiday season. small gains, mostly real, mostly clustered in the unrehearsed weeks before and after.

for you the rehearsal is different. it's not about a smaller plate. it's about staying at the table. eating with people. not running calculations under your breath.

principle = full participation. not minimization. show up the way someone who trusted her body would.

pages.headline: rehearse the *staying*
pages.body: pick the next gathering. write three sentences. arrival move. one thing you'll eat without negotiation. who you'll sit next to and stay with. presence is the practice.
