pages.headline: she goes to the *party*. fully.
pages.body: today, the rehearsal is the work. the food gets to be food. tomorrow we talk about the scale.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: butryn et al., obesity (2007)

cohortOverrides.restrictive: optional cadence — body-fit cues as backup metric.

pages.headline: the scale's *role* from here
pages.body: ten weeks ago the scale could vote on your whole day in four seconds. you've spent the program reducing its power without throwing it away, which is harder than either. now we give it the job it's qualified for. not a verdict. a recurring data point in a much larger picture.

pages.headline: frequency is *protective*. interpretation is the *risk*.
pages.body: butryn's group studied maintainers. people who weighed at least weekly were much more likely to keep weight off long term. people who stopped tended to drift. how you read the number matters too. data point or verdict, depending on the lens.
pages.citation: butryn et al., obesity (2007)
pages.data_tie: your own weight trend, not today's number, is the signal we read.

pages.headline: pick the cadence you'll *keep*
pages.body: decide your long-term weigh-in rhythm. daily and feed the trend. weekly same morning. monthly check-in. all three work if they get done. pick the one your real life will actually carry, and let the trend line do the reading for you.

pages.headline: she weighs in. it doesn't *weigh* her.
pages.body: today, the number is just a number. tomorrow we tune the regulator skills that hold this whole thing up.

pages.headline: the scale's *role* from here
pages.body: for some women the scale is a tool. for some it's a hook the old voice pulls on. you know which one yours has been. for the next stretch we're going to write you a cadence that fits the brain you actually live in, not the one a study assumes. the scale can be optional. the contact with your body isn't.

pages.headline: self-monitoring matters. the *form* gets to flex.
pages.body:
butryn and colleagues found weekly weighing was protective for many maintainers. that's one form of self-monitoring. body-fit cues are another. how clothes sit. energy through the afternoon. sleep quality. how the body feels on the floor in the morning.

the principle behind the finding is contact. people who stayed in contact with their body trajectory did better long term. the scale is one way to do that. it isn't the only way.

you get to pick the form that keeps contact without re-opening the old loop.
pages.citation: butryn et al., obesity (2007)
pages.data_tie: the trend you choose to read, in whatever form, is the data we follow.

pages.headline: choose the *contact* method
pages.body: optional cadence on the scale. monthly, never, or quietly. and one backup body-fit cue you'll actually check on. clothes, energy, how the day feels. pick the contact method that doesn't reopen the old loop.

pages.headline: she stays in *contact*. on her terms.
pages.body: today, the contact is the practice. tomorrow we tune the regulators.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: lally et al., european journal of social psychology (2010)

pages.headline: the *regulator* skills
pages.body: ten weeks in you've collected a small set of tools that sit under everything. the pause before the snack drawer. the breath that takes the edge off the meeting. the kind sentence that interrupts the spiral. these are your regulators.

pages.headline: what gets practiced gets *automatic*
pages.body: lally's group at university college london tracked how long a new behavior takes to feel automatic. the median was sixty-six days. some habits formed in three weeks. some took most of a year. what mattered was the steady context, not the streak.
pages.citation: lally et al., european journal of social psychology (2010)
pages.breath_line: two longer exhales before the next decision. that's it.

pages.headline: two long *exhales* before the next pivot
pages.body: before your next transition, the next meeting, the next meal, the next message, two slow exhales. longer out than in. you don't have to feel calm. you just have to take the two breaths. the regulator runs on reps, not vibes.
pages.breath_line: in through the nose. out twice as long. twice.

pages.headline: she has a few small tools. she *uses* them.
pages.body: today, the breath is the whole assignment. tomorrow we look at the people around you.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: christakis & fowler, new england journal of medicine (2007)

pages.headline: the social *architecture*
pages.body: the friend whose afternoon is wine in a glass before you've taken your coat off. the coworker who narrates every lunch as a confession. the group chat that runs on dieting jokes. you love them.

pages.headline: your *network* shapes the default
pages.body: christakis and fowler's framingham analysis found health behaviors cluster through social networks. you're shaped by who you eat with, and by who they eat with. not a reason to cut people. a reason to design the rooms. maintainers have a couple of clean rooms.
pages.citation: christakis & fowler, new england journal of medicine (2007)

pages.headline: name your *anchor* people
pages.body: pick two or three people who make it easier to be the maintainer version of you. text one of them today, not about the program. just contact. you're tending the rooms, not auditing them.

pages.headline: she designs the *rooms*. quietly.
pages.body: today, one message to one anchor. tomorrow we talk about the weeks where life refuses to cooperate.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: neff & germer, mindful self-compassion workbook (2018)

pages.headline: when life gets *hard*
pages.body: you woke up and the leggings fit the way they fit. and an old voice tried to make that mean something. either a win or a verdict. you have a relationship with those leggings. they've been a scale you couldn't take off. but you noticed the voice. you didn't obey it.

pages.headline: kindness is the *buffer*. not the *prize*.
pages.body: kristin neff's work consistently found that the kind voice toward yourself in hard moments isn't a prize that arrives after the work. it's the condition that lets the work continue. women who could speak to themselves like a friend during a setback had less binge eating, better adherence, lower drop-out. on a hard week, kindness isn't soft. it's structural.
pages.citation: neff & germer, mindful self-compassion workbook (2018)

pages.headline: write the *friend-sentence*
pages.body: write one line you'd say to a friend who was having the kind of week you're having. then read it back to yourself in your own voice. that's the practice. you don't have to believe it the first time. you just have to write it.
pages.prompt: to a friend going through this i would say: ___.

pages.headline: today, when she pipes up, just *thank* her and keep moving.
pages.body: she's old code. you're running new code now. tomorrow we audit what else gets to stay♡

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wing & hill, annual review of nutrition (2001)

pages.headline: if you *regain*
pages.body: three pounds back, or five, or some number that arrived without an invitation. and the old script has a monologue ready. you knew it. might as well start over from zero. but you are eleven weeks more skilled than the last time three pounds came back.

pages.headline: small regain is the *common* path. catching it is the *skill*.
pages.body: wing and hill's long-term review found something the wellness internet skips. most successful long-term maintainers regain some weight at some point. the difference between them and people who fully drift is the next month. the maintainer catches the upward shift early. she returns to her daily three. she doesn't relitigate the whole program.
pages.citation: wing & hill, annual review of nutrition (2001)
pages.data_tie: watch your trend line over weeks, not the daily number, when the drift starts.

pages.headline: write your *recovery* move
pages.body: decide now, in a calm moment, what you'll do when the trend drifts up two or three pounds for a couple weeks. not a punishment. a return. which of your daily three you'll tighten. who you'll tell. when you'll check back. quiet, written, ready.
pages.prompt: if my trend drifts up over two weeks i will: ___.

pages.headline: she has a *return* path. not a start-over.
pages.body: today, the recovery move on paper is the work. tomorrow we talk about hunger across the years to come.

pages.headline: if you *regain*
pages.body: for women with a restrictive history this lesson can pull on an old hook. so we're going to keep it small. you can skip this one if today isn't the day. for the women who want to read it, the headline is this. you are not the same brain you were the last time a number went up. the skills you carry change the meaning of the number entirely.

pages.headline: small drift is the *common* path. care is the *skill*.
pages.body:
wing and hill's long-term review of maintenance research found that most successful long-term maintainers have small drift periods. what separates them from full relapse is gentleness in the recovery, not severity.

for you the recovery move is the daily three, not new restriction. contact with the body. continued eating, not less. extra kindness in the self-talk.

the gentle return is the maintainer's actual move.
pages.citation: wing & hill, annual review of nutrition (2001)
pages.data_tie: trend over weeks, soft eye, no daily reading.

pages.headline: write the *gentle* return
pages.body: if there's drift, your return is keep eating, keep your daily three, tell one person, do not cut food. this is the skill. write it down so the old script doesn't get to author it later.
pages.prompt: if there's drift my gentle return is: ___.

pages.headline: she has a *return* path. not a start-over.
pages.body: today, kindness is the whole move. tomorrow we talk about hunger across the years.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hall et al., gastroenterology (2011)

pages.headline: hunger across the *lifespan*
pages.body: the hunger you have today isn't the hunger you'll have at thirty-five, or forty, or in the eight weeks after a baby, or in the first month off a medication. hunger has a biology and the biology shifts. the women who maintain longest learn to translate it as it changes shape.

pages.headline: the body *defends* its weight. that's biology.
pages.body: kevin hall's lab measured what happens to appetite hormones after weight loss. hunger hormones rise. fullness hormones fall. the body works to return to its prior weight, for months. not a flaw in you. biology. the skills are how you live alongside it.
pages.citation: hall et al., gastroenterology (2011)

pages.headline: respect the *signal*, run the structure
pages.body: when hunger gets louder than expected, the move isn't to override. it's to check the structure. sleep last night. protein at the last meal. movement that day. stress sitting unmetabolized. tune the inputs. the signal calms.

pages.headline: she *translates* hunger. she doesn't fight it.
pages.body: today, listen to what the signal is telling you about the inputs. tomorrow we talk about the critic that's been with you the whole time.

pages.headline: hunger across the *lifespan*
pages.body: on the medication, hunger is quiet in a way you forgot was possible. and somewhere ahead, the dose may change, or the medication may end. that's not a cliff you should be scared of. it's a transition you should plan for. the skills you've been building these twelve weeks are the structure that holds when the chemistry shifts.

pages.headline: the body *defends*. the medication *interrupts*.
pages.body:
kevin hall and colleagues at the nih have measured what happens to appetite hormones after weight loss. hunger hormones rise. fullness hormones fall. that biology applies to everyone, on or off medication.

glp-1 medication mutes hunger signaling at the brain level. when the dose changes, or the medication ends, the underlying defended-weight biology comes back online. that's expected. it's not failure.

taper prep is part of the work. the skills, the structure, the daily three were built for the days the chemistry isn't doing it for you.
pages.citation: hall et al., gastroenterology (2011)

pages.headline: build the *off-med* structure now
pages.body: while hunger is quiet, get the inputs locked. protein anchored at each meal. sleep protected. movement automatic. these are the rails the medication won't be carrying forever. you're prepping the version of you who'll live without the mute button.

pages.headline: she runs the *structure*. the medication helps.
pages.body: today, credit the medicine. and stack the skills. tomorrow we visit the critic.

pages.headline: hunger across the *lifespan*
pages.body: the body you have right now is not the one you had at twenty-eight. hunger shows up differently. fullness lasts a different length of time. sleep changes what your appetite does the next day. nothing about this is you doing the program wrong. this is the lifespan, and the maintainer learns the new chapter instead of comparing it to the old one.

pages.headline: the body *defends*. the chapter *shifts*.
pages.body:
kevin hall and colleagues at the nih have measured how appetite hormones respond to weight loss. hunger up, fullness down, for months. that's true in every chapter.

in perimenopause estrogen and sleep changes layer on top of that. appetite rhythms shift. body composition shifts. the program that worked for you at twenty-eight needs translation, not abandonment.

your body is doing exactly what bodies do at this stage. the skills are how you live with the new chapter, not how you fight it.
pages.citation: hall et al., gastroenterology (2011)

pages.headline: tune for the *current* chapter
pages.body: the inputs to watch this season. sleep more than you used to. protein anchored. movement that includes some strength. stress metabolized. the structure carries the chapter.

pages.headline: she *translates* the chapter. she doesn't grieve it.
pages.body: today, the body in front of you gets the plan. tomorrow we visit the critic.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: neff, self and identity (2003)

pages.headline: the relationship with the *critic*, ongoing
pages.body: twelve weeks in, the inner critic is still here. she didn't move out. she got quieter and less interesting, but she's still around, and on certain days she tries her old lines. the critic doesn't get evicted. you just stop subletting your day to her.

pages.headline: self-compassion is a *practice*, not a personality
pages.body: kristin neff defined self-compassion as three things. kindness toward yourself in suffering. recognition that suffering is part of being human. mindful awareness without over-identifying. her research found women higher in self-compassion show less binge eating, lower body shame, better adherence. it's trainable. you don't have to feel it. you have to practice the move.

pages.headline: one line to the *critic*
pages.body: next time she pipes up, one sentence. thank you, i hear you, i'm not taking notes. you don't have to argue. you don't have to be moved. you just have to keep walking past the kitchen counter.

pages.headline: she shares the apartment. she runs the *day*.
pages.body: today, one calm line back. tomorrow we walk through the kitchen.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: thorndike et al., american journal of public health (2012)

pages.headline: the kitchen audit *revisited*
pages.body: open the cabinet. the one over the stove or the snack drawer in the office. the version of you who set it up was a different person. she stocked for a different brain, a different relationship with the 4pm slump. a kitchen tells the truth. and you've moved.

pages.headline: the *eye-line* does the choosing
pages.body: thorndike's team rearranged the massachusetts general cafeteria so healthier choices sat at eye level. higher-friction items moved lower. no signage. over two years, behavior shifted toward the easier choices. your kitchen is your cafeteria. the eye-line shapes the default.

pages.headline: move *three* things
pages.body: tonight, three small moves. one easy food brought forward to eye level. one harder-friction item relocated. one bowl, container, or plate that lives in a different place. that's the audit. you're not throwing anything out. you're letting the room agree with you.

pages.headline: her kitchen reads like her *now*.
pages.body: today, three moves. that's enough. tomorrow we talk about the body that keeps changing.

pages.headline: the kitchen audit *revisited*
pages.body: open the cabinet. for women with a restrictive history this room can carry a lot. so we're going to do the audit with a soft hand. the principle here is relocation, not removal. nothing leaves the house. some things just move around so the brain doesn't have to negotiate them every afternoon.

pages.headline: *relocate*, don't remove
pages.body:
thorndike and colleagues at massachusetts general hospital found that small changes to eye-level and access in a cafeteria nudged behavior over two years without removing any options. for you the principle is identical with the safeguard built in. removal can reinstate the scarcity loop. relocation just adjusts friction.

all food stays available. you're not in restriction. you're in design.

the room is allowed to support you without becoming a rule.
pages.citation: thorndike et al., american journal of public health (2012)

pages.headline: move *three* things, keep them all
pages.body: tonight, three small moves. one easy food brought forward. one item relocated, not removed. one plate or bowl in a new spot. you're tuning, not policing.

pages.headline: her kitchen reads like her *now*.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: tylka & wood-barcalow, body image (2015)

pages.headline: the body that keeps *changing*
pages.body: you stood in front of the mirror this morning and the body looking back at you isn't the body from week one, and it isn't the body you'll have at fifty. the body keeps changing for the rest of your life. the maintainer's work isn't freezing a moment.

pages.headline: *neutrality* travels further than confidence
pages.body: tracy tylka's research separated real body appreciation from forced positivity. forced positivity collapses under stress. body appreciation, a calmer respect for the body that performs for you, holds across life stages. the woman who keeps a kind relationship with her body for forty years isn't the one who feels great every morning. she returns to neutrality faster.
pages.citation: tylka & wood-barcalow, body image (2015)

pages.headline: the *appreciation* sentence

pages.headline: she stays in *conversation*. across years.

pages.headline: the body that keeps *changing*
pages.body: the body in the mirror is in a chapter you didn't pick the timeline for. fat distributes differently. sleep affects everything. things that worked last year don't land the same way now. all of this is real and none of it is your fault. you're allowed to grieve the chapter you left without grieving yourself.

pages.headline: *neutrality* travels further than confidence
pages.body:
tylka and wood-barcalow's body image research found that body appreciation, a calmer respect for the body that lives for you, holds up across life stages. in midlife the body is doing exactly what bodies do. the work is not to fight that. the work is to return to neutrality after the harder mornings.

your body is changing on biology's timeline. your relationship with it doesn't have to.

pages.headline: the *appreciation* sentence

pages.headline: the body that keeps *changing*
pages.body: the body that grew a person is in front of you. it's tired and it's miraculous and it's not the body from before, and it isn't supposed to be. the recovery has its own clock. you're allowed to want strength and to also let this season be what it is. nothing about this body needs to be raced.

pages.headline: *neutrality* travels further than confidence
pages.body:
tylka and wood-barcalow's body image research found that body appreciation, a calmer respect for the body that performs and lives for you, holds up across life stages. postpartum is one of the most demanding chapters a body runs. the research-backed move is gentleness now, return to neutrality faster on the hard days, and resist any culture that tells you to undo the chapter.

the return is the skill. the timeline is yours.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: forman et al., obesity (2016)

pages.headline: values as your *compass*
pages.body: twelve weeks ago you started this for a reason. probably more than one. maybe a number. maybe a wedding. maybe a feeling you remembered having in your body. those reasons were a starting line. they weren't enough to live a maintenance life on.

pages.headline: values are the *direction*. goals are *checkpoints*.
pages.body: forman's group at drexel tested acceptance-based treatment for weight loss, with values clarification at the center. at three-year follow-up, participants anchored to a values direction maintained more loss than ones anchored to goal numbers. goals are destinations.
pages.citation: forman et al., obesity (2016)

pages.headline: name *three* values
pages.body: not three goals. three directions. what do you want your relationship with your body, your food, your energy to be moving toward. not how much, not by when. one or two words each.
pages.prompt: my three directions: 1) ___ 2) ___ 3) ___.

pages.headline: she walks the *direction*. the destinations come.
pages.body: today, three words on paper. tomorrow we talk about every january.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: mann et al., american psychologist (2007)

pages.headline: the every-january *test*
pages.body: every january the internet starts shouting. new program. new before-and-after. new version of you on the other side of six weeks of being smaller. and a small part of you, even after all this, will perk up and listen. that perking-up isn't failure. it's culture.

pages.headline: the *recycle* rate is the part they don't print
pages.body: traci mann's group reviewed long-term outcomes from dozens of diet studies. most dieters regained within a few years, and a meaningful portion ended up heavier than they started. not a finding against trying. against the same architecture. the january program is selling what you already left. the test isn't noticing the ad. it's whether you click.
pages.citation: mann et al., american psychologist (2007)

pages.headline: write your *january line*
pages.body: one line you'll say to yourself when the next big diet announcement scrolls past you. something honest, specific, yours. not a slogan. a sentence that reminds you what you already know about how this works.
pages.prompt: when the next program promises a new me i'll say: ___.

pages.headline: she scrolls past. she's *busy* living.
pages.body: today, the january line is the work. tomorrow we tune the recommit skill.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: thomas et al., american journal of preventive medicine (2014)

pages.headline: the *recommit* skill
pages.body: you took three days off the rhythm and tuesday morning the brain tried to make it a thing. a clean slate. a full restart. a monday of mondays. the maintainer has a quieter move. she opens the door and walks back in. no entry music. no apology. she picks up where she left off.

pages.headline: the *return* is the whole skill
pages.body: thomas's group followed registry members for ten years. one of the most stable predictors of continued maintenance wasn't the perfect day. it was the time between a drift and the return. maintainers who lasted had shorter slips. they returned at the next meal.
pages.citation: thomas et al., american journal of preventive medicine (2014)
pages.breath_line: one breath in. one longer out. resume.

pages.headline: the *next-meal* recommit
pages.body: next time the rhythm drops, one breath, longer exhale, and the next meal is where you return. not monday. not after one more wild night. the next meal. that's the entire skill, repeated over a lifetime.
pages.breath_line: in slow. out longer. back at the next plate.

pages.headline: she *returns*. quietly. on time.
pages.body: today, the breath and the next meal. tomorrow we write the long-form plan.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: the *lifetime* plan
pages.body: today you write a small document. not a contract. not a vow. a one-page plan you can hand to the future version of yourself who, six months from now, on a wednesday, will need a quiet reminder of what she already knows. not because they forget. because the room sometimes does.

pages.headline: the *written* plan does the remembering for you
pages.body: cooper, fairburn and hawker put a written maintenance plan at the literal end of treatment for a reason. people who walked out with a plan in their own words maintained better than people with the same skills only in their head. the writing externalizes the work. on a hard week you don't have to remember everything. you find the document.
pages.citation: cooper, fairburn, hawker, cognitive-behavioral treatment of obesity (2003)

pages.headline: write your *one-page* plan
pages.body: five lines. my daily three. my top three high-risk situations and pre-decided moves. my recommit move. my anchor people. my one-line january script. keep it somewhere you'll find without searching. that's the artifact.
pages.prompt: my lifetime plan: 1) daily three ___ 2) high-risk + moves ___ 3) recommit ___ 4) anchors ___ 5) january line ___.

pages.headline: she carries the *page*. it carries her back.
pages.body: today, the page is the work. tomorrow we look at the quiet woman you've become.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: hayes, strosahl & wilson, act 2nd ed (2011)

pages.headline: the *quiet* identity
pages.body: twelve weeks ago you were loud about it. the program. the way you were going to different. somewhere it got quieter. you stopped narrating. on a wednesday in week eleven you ordered the thing you wanted, didn't run a calculation, and didn't notice until thursday.

pages.headline: you stopped *being* the script. you started *holding* it.
pages.body: in ACT, hayes describes self-as-context. instead of being fused with the thoughts you have, you notice yourself as the larger space where thoughts arrive and pass. you have the script. you are not the script. it shows up as the day you noticed an old thought, didn't argue, didn't obey, and kept doing the dishes. the noticing is the new identity.
pages.citation: hayes, strosahl & wilson, act 2nd ed (2011)

pages.headline: notice one *quiet* moment
pages.body: today, look for the moment where the old script tried to load and you just kept moving. you don't have to celebrate it. you don't have to tell anyone. just see it. that moment is the new identity in its everyday clothes.

pages.headline: she's already *her*. she just got quiet about it.
pages.body: today, see the quiet. tomorrow we talk about what stays.

canonical84.evidenceAnchor: wadden et al., look ahead trial (2014)

pages.headline: what stays after the *app*
pages.body: twelve weeks ago you opened this on a tuesday night. tonight is also a tuesday. or it isn't. either way, the woman closing this lesson isn't the same one who opened the first. you didn't become someone else. you learned. and the learning is yours now. it doesn't live in the app.

pages.headline: the *skills* travel. the program *ends*.
pages.body: the look ahead trial followed adults through a behavioral weight program, then a decade after. the women who maintained most took specific skills with them. self-monitoring at a workable cadence. planning ahead. social support. self-compassion. none required the program. what stays is what you practice.
pages.citation: wadden et al., look ahead trial (2014)

pages.headline: write *what stays*
pages.body: one last short list. three things from these twelve weeks that you'll carry past today. not the favorites. the load-bearing ones. the small moves you can already feel running underneath your week without permission.
pages.prompt: three things that stay: 1) ___ 2) ___ 3) ___.

pages.headline: she *held*. she *kept*. she *returned*. she *recommitted*.
pages.body: today is small. and kind of *everything*. you came in skeptical and you're leaving skilled. tomorrow resets the way it always does. you already know what to do♡

voicePlaybook.north_star: Jeni speaks like the smartest girlfriend you've ever had — the one who read every study, did the therapy, dated the wrong nutritionist, and came back with the real intel. She talks lowercase, lands one italic punch word per line, and never performs expertise; she just hands it to you like it's already yours. The register is post-Ozempic, post-wellness, post-shame — fluent in food noise and satiety, allergic to crush-it gym-bro labor verbs and TED-talk AI vocabulary. Stories open in specific physical scenes — the 11pm pantry door, the sunday weigh-in, the 2:47pm snack drawer — never in abstraction. Closes return her dignity and hand off to tomorrow.
sentence_rhythm_exemplars: okay so the *first* thing nobody tells you about food noise.
your body isn't broken. the script in your head is.
today is small. it's also kind of *everything*.
the thought isn't the truth. it's a habit your brain practiced.
you don't have to feel like her yet. you just have to move like her *once*.
the urge is a wave, not a prophecy. surf 90 seconds and see what's still true.

gold_standard_story_close_pairs.lesson_context: Act I W1 D2 — naming the food-noise voice
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.story: it's 11pm and you're standing in front of the open pantry. the apartment is quiet in that specific way that makes thoughts louder. you're not hungry. you know you're not hungry. but there's a voice doing math at you. how many calories you had. what tomorrow has to look like. whether the cereal counts if you eat it standing up. that voice has a *name*, and we're going to learn it tonight. it's not you. it never was.
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.close: tonight, if she shows up, just *notice* her. don't argue. don't obey. just clock that she's there, like a roommate you didn't pick. tomorrow we give her a name.

gold_standard_story_close_pairs.lesson_context: Act I W2 D11 — the sunday weigh-in as cognitive distortion
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.story: sunday morning. hair still doing whatever it does. you step on the scale before coffee, before anyone, before you've even decided what kind of person you are today. the number comes up. and in the space of about four seconds, you decide what the whole week gets to feel like. one number. seven days. that's a *trade* your brain made without asking you, and today we're going to interrupt it.
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.close: next sunday, you can still weigh in. that's fine. but you don't have to let the number *vote* on your week. tomorrow we'll talk about who actually gets the vote.

gold_standard_story_close_pairs.lesson_context: Act II W4 D27 — building the urge plan
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.story: your hand is already on the office snack drawer. it's 2:47, you're not hungry, and a deeply boring spreadsheet is open behind you. the drawer is reliable. the drawer is *kind*, almost. it doesn't ask anything of you. but somewhere between the thought and the open-the-drawer, there's a window. small. maybe five seconds. most of your life you've been speedrunning past it. this week we're going to live inside it.
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.close: today, just try to *feel* the window. you don't have to use it yet. you just have to know it's there. tomorrow we'll practice stretching it open.

gold_standard_story_close_pairs.lesson_context: Act III W7 D43 — identity vs goal
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.story: you've spent years being the friend who's 'working on it.' the one with the food rules. the one who orders the salad and then talks about ordering the salad. it became a *personality*. and somewhere underneath all the working-on-it, there's a question you've been avoiding. who would you be, in a body you weren't trying to escape? we're going to sit with that one for a minute. not solve it. just sit.
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.close: you don't have to answer the question yet. you just have to let it *exist* in the room with you. that's the whole thing. tomorrow we'll start writing toward it♡

gold_standard_story_close_pairs.lesson_context: Act IV W11 D73 — when life gets hard
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.story: you woke up and the leggings fit the way they fit. and a small voice, an old voice, tried to make that *mean* something. either a win or a verdict. you have a relationship with those leggings now. they've been a scale you couldn't take off. but here's what's new, and we're going to honor it. you noticed the voice. you didn't obey it. that's eleven weeks of work, doing the quietest possible thing.
gold_standard_story_close_pairs.close: today, when she pipes up, just *thank* her and keep moving. she's old code. you're running new code now. tomorrow we audit what else gets to stay♡

italic_fraunces_punch_placement.rule: ONE punch word per line, max 2 per paragraph. Three placements: (1) the verb that does the cognitive reframe — *notice*, *name*, *catch*, *pause*, *clock*, *interrupt*. (2) The noun that renames the thing — *voice*, *window*, *roommate*, *trade*, *data point*. (3) The emotional pivot word in the close — *new*, *resets*, *pause*, *fix*, *thank*, *proud*.
italic_fraunces_punch_placement.forbidden: italic on filler ('it's *just*'), italic on three+ words in a line, italic markers via asterisk in body strings (italic comes through AttributedString builder only). No double-italic in same sentence.

anti_shame_absolutes: Never use diet-culture verbs: crush, shred, burn, earn, deficit, sin, cheat, bad, naughty, indulge, splurge. Food does not require moral defense.
Never reference 'beach body,' 'bikini body,' 'snap back,' 'pre-baby body,' 'real you waiting' — any frame that positions current body as deviation from truer body.
Never put a number on a body the user hasn't opted to share. All numerics tie to user's own collected fields or are dropped.
Never frame GLP-1 as cheating, easy mode, shortcut, or 'when you go off it the real work starts.' It is medicine. Credit the medicine.
Never imply the user's prior attempts failed because of her. Attempts fail because diet culture sells a model that does not work. She did not fail diets. Diets failed her.
Never tie self-worth or being-loved-by-others to weight, body shape, or program adherence. The lesson collapses if a missed day reads as a missed self.
Never use before/after framing inside lessons. Progress shows via her own collected data trend, never implied transformation arc.
Never use 'should,' 'must,' 'have to' as the lesson's directive verb. Use 'try,' 'notice,' 'see what happens when.' Collaborator, not patient.
Never end a lesson on a problem. Every lesson closes on a next-bite-sized action and a sentence that returns her dignity.
Em-dash and double-hyphen between words are BANNED. Glyph '—' as no-data placeholder stays OK; never in prose.

perActSignature.animation_emphasis: Act I (Deconstruct) is the bare register. Treatments lean typographyOnly + singleArtifact; grain breathing period 1.6s; Motion.entrance default. The act is about removing things — the page should feel like air. Body word target 45-65 per page, headline 38pt, tight leading (lineSpacing 6).

Act II (Build) earns the anchored register. singleArtifact density rises, photoEdgeBleed enters; grain breathing 1.7s; kicker thickens by one weight to signal accumulation. Body 65-95 words, headline 36pt, loose leading (lineSpacing 9). Things are accumulating with intention.

Act III (Rewire) goes intimate. Paper tint warms ~2% toward peach via shader uniform; heroPhotoBleed enters the rotation; gradientOrbSelection appears for self-relational picks; grain breathing slows to 1.9s; Motion.entranceSoft default. Body 40-60 words, headline 40pt, tight leading. Compression as intensity.

Act IV (Maintain) returns to canonical cream with brighter chapter dots. Grain breathing returns to 1.6s; Motion.entrance default; drop-caps appear as the graduation typographic ornament. Body 70-110 words, headline 34pt, loose leading. Maintenance is undramatic by design — the page models the spaciousness of a maintained life.
